    "https:\/\/braindump.jethro.dev\/posts\/statistical_learning\/": {
        
        "title": "Statistical Learning",
        "tags": [],
        "content": "Introduction Statistical learning refers to a vast set of tools for understanding data. It involves building a statistical model for predicting, or estimating, an output based on one or more input.\nOur goal is to apply a statistical learning method to the training data in order to estimate the unknown function \\(f\\). Broadly speaking, most statistical learning methods for this task can be characterized as either parametric or non-parametric.\nParametric Methods Parametric methods involve a two-step model-based approach:\n First, we make an assumption about the functional form, or shape, of \\(f\\). For example, one simple assumption is that \\(f\\) is linear in \\(X\\):  \\begin{equation} f(X) = \\beta_0 + \\beta_1X_1 + \\dots + \\beta_p X_p \\end{equation}\nThis is a linear model, and with this assumption the problem of estimating \\(f\\) is greatly simplified. Instead of estimating an arbitrary p-dimensional function \\(f(X)\\), one only needs to estimate the \\(p+1\\) coefficients \\(\\beta_0, \\dots, \\beta_p\\).\nAfter a model has been selected, we need a procedure that uses the training data to fit or train the model. In the case of the linear model, we need to estimate the parameters \\(\\beta_0, \\beta_1, \\dots, \\beta_p\\). The most common approach to fitting the model is referred to as the (ordinary) least squares.\nThe model-based approach just described is referred to as parametric; it reduces the problem of estimating \\(f\\) down to one of estimating a set of parameters. The potential disadvantage of a parametric approach is that the model we choose will usually not match the true unknown form of \\(f\\). If the chosen model is too far from the true \\(f\\), then our estimate will be poor. Flexible models require a large amount of parameters, and complex models are also susceptible to overfitting.\nNon-parametric Methods Non-parametric methods do not make explicit assumptions about the functional form of \\(f\\). Instead, they seek an estimate of \\(f\\) that gets as close to the data points as possible without being too rough or wiggly. By avoiding assumptions of a particular form of \\(f\\), non-parametric approaches can possibly fit the wider range of possible shapes of \\(f\\). However, they do not reduce the problem of estimating \\(f\\) to a small number of parameters, and a large number of observations is required to obtain an accurate estimate for \\(f\\). An example of a non-parametric model is the thin-plate spline.\nThe Trade-Off Between Prediction Accuracy and Model Interpretability   Figure 1: A representation of the tradeoff between flexibility and interpretability, using different statistical methods.\n  There are several reasons to choose a restrictive model over a flexible approach. First, if the interest is mainly in inference, restrictive models tend to be much more interpretable. For example linear models make it easy to understand the associations between individual predictors and the response. Even when predictions are the only concern, highly flexible models are susceptible to overfitting, and restrictive models can often outperform them.\nMeasuring Quality of Fit In order to evaluate the performance of a statistical learning method on a given data set, we need some way to measure how well its predictions actually match the observed data.\nIn the regression setting, the most commonly-used measure is the mean squared error (MSE), given by:\n\\begin{equation} \\label{eqn:dfn:mse} \\mathrm{MSE} = \\frac{1}{n} \\mathop{\\sum}_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \\hat{f}(x_i))^2 \\end{equation}\nThe MSE will be small if the predicted responses are very close to the true responses.\nBias-Variance Trade-Off It can be shown taht the expected test MSE, for a given value \\(x_0\\), can always be decomposed into the sum of 3 fundamental qualities: the variance of \\(\\hat{f}(x_0)\\), the squared bias of \\(\\hat{f}(x_0)\\), and the variance of the error terms \\(\\epsilon\\):\n\\begin{equation} \\label{eqn:bvtrade} \\mathrm{E} (y_0 - \\hat{f}(x_0))^2 = \\mathrm{Var}(\\hat{f}(x_0)) + \\left[ \\mathrm{Bias}(\\hat{f}(x_0)) \\right]^2 + \\mathrm{Var}(\\epsilon) \\end{equation}\nThe expected test MSE refers to the average test MSE that we would obtain if we repeatedly estimated \\(f\\) using a large number of training sets, and tested each at \\(x_0\\).\nThe variance of a statistical learning method refers to the amount by which \\(\\hat{f}\\) would change if we estimated it using a different training data set. Since the training data are used to fit the statistical learning method, different training data sets will result in a different \\(\\hat{f}\\). In general, more flexible statistical methods have higher variance.\nBias refers to the error that is introduced by approximating a real-life problem, which may be extremely complicated, by a much simpler model. In general, more flexible approaches have lower bias.\nThe Bayes Classifier When the test error rate is minimized, the classifier assigns each observation to the most likely class, given its predictor values. This is known as the Bayes classifier. The Bayes classifier produces the lowest possible error rate, known as the Bayes error rate, given by:\n\\begin{equation} \\label{eqn:bayes_error_rate} 1 - \\mathrm{E} \\left( \\mathop{\\mathrm{max}}_{j} \\mathrm{Pr}(Y = j | X) \\right) \\end{equation}\nIn theory, we would always like to predict qualitative responses using the Bayes classifier. However, for real data, we do not know the conditional distribution of \\(Y\\) given \\(X\\), and computing the Bayes classifier would be impossible.\nK-Nearest Neighbours The Bayes classifier serves as the unattainable gold standard against which to compare other methods. Many approaches attempt to estimate the conditional distribution of \\(Y\\) given \\(X\\), and then classify the given observation to the class with highest estimated probability. One such method is the K-nearest neighbours classifier.\nGiven a positive integer \\(K\\) and a test observation \\(x_0\\), the KNN classifier first identifies the K points in the training data that are closest to \\(x_0\\), represented by \\(N_0\\). It then estimates the conditional probability for class \\(j\\) as the fraction of points in \\(N_0\\) whose response values equal \\(j\\):\n\\begin{equation} \\label{eqn:knn} \\mathrm{Pr}(Y=j | X = x_0) = \\frac{1}{K} \\sum_{i\\in N_0}I(y_i = j) \\end{equation}\nFinally, KNN applies Bayes rule and classifies the test observation \\(x_0\\) to the class with the largest probability.\nThe Statistical Learning Framework Consider the problem of classifying a papaya into 2 bins: tasty or not tasty. We\u0026rsquo;ve chosen 2 features:\n The papaya\u0026rsquo;s colour, ranging from dark green through orange and red to dark brown The papaya\u0026rsquo;s softness, ranging from rock hard to mushy  The learner\u0026rsquo;s input consists of:\n  Domain set: An arbitrary set, \\(\\mathcal{X}\\). This is the set of objects that we may wish to label. The domain set in our example will be the set of all papayas. Usually, these domain represented by a vector of features (like colour and softness). We also refer to domain points as instances and to \\(\\mathcal{X}\\) as the instance space.\n  Label set: The label set is restricted in our example to a two-element set, usually {0, 1} or {-1, +1}.\n  Training data: \\(S = ((x_1, y_1) \\dots (x_m, y_m))\\) is a finite sequence of pairs in \\(\\mathcal{X} \\times \\mathcal{Y}\\). This is the input that the learner has access to. Such labeled examples are often called training examples. \\(S\\) is also sometimes referred to as the training set.\n  The learner\u0026rsquo;s output: The learner is requested to output a prediction rule \\(h: \\mathcal{X} \\rightarrow \\mathcal{Y}\\). This function is also called a predictor, a hypothesis, or a classifier. The predictor can be used to predict the label of new domain points.\n  A simple data-generation model: This explains how the training data is generated. First, we assume that the instances are generated by some probability distribution. We denote the probability distribution over \\(\\mathcal{X}\\) by \\(\\mathcal{D}\\). we do not assume that the learner knows anything about this distribution.\n  Measures of success: We define the error of a classifier to be the probability that it does not predict the correct label on a random data point generated by the underlying distribution. That is, the error of \\(h\\) is the probability to draw a random instance \\(x\\) from \\(\\mathcal{D}\\), such that \\(h(x) \\ne f(x)\\), where \\(f(x)\\) is the true labelling function:\n\\begin{equation} \\label{eqn:dfn:error} L_{\\mathcal{D}, f} (h) \\overset{\\mathrm{def}}{=} \\mathop{P}_{x \\sim \\mathcal{D}} \\left[ h(x) \\ne f(x) \\right] \\overset{\\mathrm{def}}{=} \\mathcal{D} (\\{ x: h(x) \\ne f(x) \\} ) \\end{equation}\n  The learner is blind to the underlying probability distribution \\(\\mathcal{D}\\) over the world, and to the labelling function \\(f\\). The learner can only interact with the environment through the training set.\nClassification The linear regression model assumes that the response variable \\(Y\\) is quantitative. However, in many cases the response variable is qualitative. Classification encompasses approaches that predict qualitative responses. 3 of the most widely-used classifiers include: logistic regression, linear discriminant analysis, and K-nearest neighbours. More computer-intensive methods include generalized additive models, trees, random forests, boosting, and support vector machines.\nWhy not Linear Regression? Encoding non-binary categorical variables as a dummy variable using integers can lead to a unwanted encoding of a relationship between the different options. With binary outcomes, linear regression does do a good job as a classifier: in fact, it is equivalent to linear discriminant analysis.\nSuppose we encode the outcome \\(Y\\) as follows:\n\\begin{equation} Y = \\begin{cases} 0 \u0026amp; \\text{if No} \\\\\\\n1 \u0026amp; \\text{if Yes} \\\\\\\n\\end{cases} \\end{equation}\nThen the population \\(E(Y|X = x) = \\mathrm{Pr}(Y=1|X=x)\\), which may seem to imply that regression is perfect for the task. However, linear regression may produce probabilities less than zero or bigger than one, hence logistic regression is more appropriate.\nLogistic Regression Rather than modelling the response \\(Y\\) directly, logistic regression models the probability that \\(Y\\) belongs to a particular category.\nHow should we model the relationship between \\(p(X) = \\mathrm{Pr}(Y=1|X)\\) and \\(X\\)? In the linear regression model, we used the formula:\n\\begin{equation} p(X) = \\beta_0 + \\beta_1 X \\end{equation}\nThis model for \\(p(X)\\) is not suitable because any time a straight line is fit to a binary response that is coded as a 0 or 1, in principle we can always predict \\(p(X) \u0026lt; 0\\) for some values of \\(X\\), and \\(p(X) \u0026gt; 1\\) for others.\nIn logistic regression, we use the logistic function:\n\\begin{equation} p(X) = \\frac{e^{\\beta_0 + \\beta_1 X}}{1 + e^{\\beta_0 + \\beta_1 X}} \\end{equation}\nThis restricts values of \\(p(X)\\) to be between 0 and 1. A bit of rearrangement gives:\n\\begin{equation} \\log \\left( \\frac{p(X)}{1-p(X)} \\right) = \\beta_0 + \\beta_1 X \\end{equation}\nAnd this monotone transformation is called the log odds or logit transformation of \\(p(X)\\).\nWe use maximum likelihood to estimate the parameters:\n\\begin{equation} l(\\beta_0, \\beta) = \\prod_{i:y_i=1} p(x_i) \\prod_{i:y_i=0} (1 - p(x_i)) \\end{equation}\nThis likelihood gives the probability of the observed zeros and ones in the data. We pick \\(\\beta_0\\) and \\(\\beta_1\\) to maximize the likelihood of the observed data.\nAs with linear regression, we can compute the coefficient values, the standard error of the coefficients, the z-statistic, and the p-value. The z-statistic plays the same role as the t-statistic. A large absolute value of the z-statistic indicates evidence against the null hypothesis.\nMultiple Logistic Regression It is easy to generalize the formula to multiple logistic regression:\n\\begin{equation} \\log \\left( \\frac{p(X)}{1-p(X)} \\right) = \\beta_0 + \\beta_1 X_1 + \\dots + \\beta_p X_p \\end{equation}\n\\begin{equation} p(X) = \\frac{e^{\\beta_0 + \\beta_1X_1 + \\dots + \\beta_pX_p}}{1 + e^{\\beta_0 + \\beta_1X_1 + \\dots + \\beta_pX_p}} \\end{equation}\nSimilarly, we use the maximum likelihood method to estimate the coefficient.\nTODO Case Control Sampling Case control sampling is most effective when the prior probabilities of the classes are very unequal.\nLinear Discriminant Analysis Logistic regression involves directly modelling \\(\\mathrm{Pr}(Y=k|X=x)\\) using the logistic function. We now consider an alternative and less direct approach to estimating these probabilities. We model the distribution of the predictors \\(X\\) separately in each of the response classes (i.e. given \\(Y\\)), and then use Bayes\u0026rsquo; theorem to flip these around into estimates for \\(\\mathrm{Pr}(Y=k|X=x)\\).\nWhen these distributions are assumed to be normal, it turns out that the model is very similar in form to logistic regression.\nWhy do we need another method?\n When the classes are well-separated, the parameter estimates for the logistic regression model are surprisingly unstable. LDA does not suffer from this issue. If n is small, and the distribution of the predictors \\(X\\) is approximately normal in each of the classes, the LDA model is more stable than the logistic regression model. LDA is more popular when we have more than 2 response classes.  We first state Bayes\u0026rsquo; theorem, and write it differently for discriminant analysis:\n\\begin{equation} {eqn:dfn:bayes} \\mathrm{Pr}(Y=k|X=x) = \\frac{\\mathrm{Pr}(X=x|Y=k) \\cdot \\mathrm{Pr}(Y=k)}{\\mathrm{Pr}(X=x)} \\end{equation}\n\\begin{equation} \\mathrm{Pr}(Y=k|X=x) = \\frac{\\pi_k f_k(x)}{\\sum_{l=1}^{K}\\pi_lf_l(x)} \\end{equation}\nwhere \\(f_k(x) = \\mathrm{Pr}(X=x|Y=k)\\) is the density for \\(X\\) in class \\(k\\), and \\(\\pi_k = \\mathrm{Pr}(Y=k)\\) is the prior probability for class \\(k\\).\nWe first discuss LDA when \\(p = 1\\). The Gaussian density has the form:\n\\begin{equation} f_k(x) = \\frac{1}{\\sqrt{2\\pi}\\sigma_k}e^{2\\frac{1}{2}\\left( \\frac{x-\\mu_k}{\\sigma_k} \\right)^2} \\end{equation}\nWe can plug this into Bayes formula and get a complicated expression for \\(p(x)\\). To classify at the value \\(X = x\\), we just need to see which of \\(p_k(x)\\) is largest. Taking logs, and discarding terms that do not depend on \\(k\\), we see that this is equivalent to assigning \\(x\\) to the class with the largest discriminant score:\n\\begin{equation} \\partial_k(x) = x \\cdot \\frac{numerator}{\\mu_k}{\\sigma^2} - \\frac{\\mu_k^2}{2\\sigma^2}+ \\log(\\pi_k) \\end{equation}\nNote that \\(\\partial_k(x)\\) is a linear function of \\(x\\). If there are \\(K=2\\) classes, and \\(\\pi_1 = \\pi_2 = 0.5\\), we can see that the decision boundary becomes \\(x = \\frac{\\mu_1 + \\mu_2}{2}\\).\nWe can estimate the parameters:\n\\begin{equation} \\hat{\\pi_k} = \\frac{n_k}{n} \\end{equation}\n\\begin{equation} \\hat{\\mu_k} = \\frac{1}{n_k}\\sum_{i:y_i=k}x_i \\end{equation}\n\\begin{equation} \\hat{\\sigma}^2 = \\frac{1}{n-K}\\sum_{k=1}^{K}\\sum_{i:y_i=k} (x_i - \\hat{\\mu_k})^2 \\end{equation}\nWe can extend Linear Discriminant Analysis to the case of multiple predictors. To do that, we will assume that \\(X = (X_1, X_2, \\dots, X_p)\\) is drawn from a multivariate Gaussian distribution, with a class-specific mean vector and a common covariance matrix.\nThe multivariate Gaussian distribution assumes that each individual predictor follows a one-dimensional normal distribution, with some correlation between each pair of predictors. Formally, the multivariate Gaussian density is defined as:\n\\begin{equation} f(x) = \\frac{1}{(2\\pi)^{p/2|\\Sigma|^{1/2}}} \\mathrm{exp} \\left( -\\frac{1}{2}(x - \\mu)^T \\Sigma^{-1}(x - \\mu) \\right) \\end{equation}\nIn the case of \\(p \u0026gt; 1\\) predictors, the LDA classifier assumes that the observations in the kith class are drawn from a multivariate Gaussian distribution \\(N(\\mu_k, \\Sigma)\\), where \\(\\Sigma\\) is common to all classes. With find that the Bayes classifier assigns an observation \\(X = x\\) to the class for which:\n\\begin{equation} \\sigma_k(x) = x^T \\Sigma^{-1}\\mu_k - \\frac{1}{2}\\mu_k^T\\Sigma^{-1}\\mu_k + \\log \\pi_k \\end{equation}\nThe LDA model has the lowest error rate the Gaussian model is correct, since it approximates the Bayes classifier. However, misclassifications can still happen, and a good way to visualize them is through a confusion matrix. The probability threshold can also be tweaked to reduce the error rates for incorrect classification to a single class.\nThe ROC (Receiver Operating Characteristics) curve is a popular graphic for simultaneously displaying the two types of errors for all possible thresholds. An ideal ROC curve will hug the top left corner, so the larger the AUC (Area Under Curve) the better the classifier. The overall performance of a classifier, summarized over all possible thresholds, is given by this value.\nVarying the classifier threshold also changes its true positive and false negative rate. These are also called the sensitivity, and 1 - specificity of the classifier.\nQuadratic Discriminant Analysis In LDA with multiple predictors, we assumed that observations are drawn from a multivariate Gaussian distribution with a class-specific mean vector and a common covariance matrix. Quadratic Discriminant Analysis (QDA) assumes that each class has its own covariance matrix. Under this assumption, the Bayes classifier assigns an observation \\(X = x\\) to the class for which:\n\\begin{equation} \\partial_k(x) = -\\frac{1}{2}(x-\\mu_k)^T \\Sigma_k^{-1}(x - \\mu_k) - \\frac{1}{2} \\log |\\Sigma_k| + \\log \\pi_k \\end{equation}\nWhen would one prefer LDA to QDA, or vice-versa? The answer lies in the bias-variance trade-off. When there are \\(p\\) predictors, estimating a covariance matrix requires estimating \\(p(p+1)/2\\) variables. In QDA with \\(K\\) predictors, we need to estimate \\(Kp(p+1)/2\\) parameters, which can quickly get big. Hence LDA is much less flexible, and has a lower variance. On the other hand, if the assumption of a common covariance matrix is bad, then LDA will perform poorly.\nComparison of Classification Methods Logistic Regression and LDA produce linear decision boundaries. The only difference between the two approaches is that in logistic regression the coefficients are estimated using maximum likelihood, while in LDA the coefficients are approximated via the estimated mean and variance from a normal distribution.\nSince logistic regression and LDA differ only in their fitting procedures, one might expect the two approaches to give similar results. Logistic regression can outperform LDA if the Gaussian assumptions are not met. On the other hand, LDA can show improvements over logistic regression if they are.\nKNN takes a completely different approach from the classifiers seen in this chapter. In order to make a prediction for an observation \\(X = x\\) , the \\(K\\) training observations that are closest to \\(x\\) are identified. Then \\(X\\) is assigned to the class to which the plurality of these observations belong. Hence KNN is a completely non-parametric approach: no assumptions are made about the shape of the decision boundary. KNN does not tell us which predictors are important, but can outperform LDA and logistic regression if the decision boundary is highly non-linear.\nThough not as flexible as the KNN, QDA can perform better in the presence of a limited number of training observations, because it does make some assumptions about the form of the decision boundary.\nReference Textbooks  An introduction to statistical learning (James et al., n.d.) Understanding Machine Learning (Shalev-Shwartz and Ben-David, n.d.)  Bibliography James, Gareth, Daniela Witten, Trevor Hastie, and Robert Tibshirani. n.d. An Introduction to Statistical Learning. Vol. 112. Springer.\nShalev-Shwartz, Shai, and Shai Ben-David. n.d. Understanding Machine Learning: From Theory to Algorithms. Cambridge university press.\n", 
        "url": "https:\/\/braindump.jethro.dev\/posts\/statistical_learning\/"
    },
    
    
    
    "https:\/\/braindump.jethro.dev\/posts\/statistical_mechanics_in_financial_markets\/": {
        
        "title": "Statistical Mechanics in Financial Markets",
        "tags": [],
        "content": "Statistical physics describes the complex behaviour of physical systems that arise from simple interaction laws.\nBecause a significant fraction of modern markets are largely traded using computer programs and not human operators, the parallels between statistical physics and modern markets are now stronger than ever.\n", 
        "url": "https:\/\/braindump.jethro.dev\/posts\/statistical_mechanics_in_financial_markets\/"
    },
    
    
    
    "https:\/\/braindump.jethro.dev\/posts\/statistical_physics_in_financial_markets\/": {

    "https:\/\/braindump.jethro.dev\/posts\/statistical_methods_for_finance\/": {
        
        "title": "Statistical Methods for Finance",
        "tags": [],
        "content": " tags Finance  Returns Net Returns:\n\\begin{equation} R_t = \\frac{P_t}{P_{t-1}} - 1 = \\frac{P_t - P_{t-1}}{P_{t-1}} \\end{equation}\n\\(R_t \\ge -1\\): a 100% loss occurs if the asset becomes totally worthless.\nGross Returns:\n\\begin{equation} \\frac{P_t}{P_{t-1}} = 1 + R_t \\end{equation}\nThe gross return over the most recent \\(k\\) periods is:\n\\begin{equation} 1 + R_t(k) = \\frac{P_t}{P_{t-k}} = (1 + R_t)\\dots (1 + R_{t-k+1}) \\end{equation}\nLog Returns:\n\\begin{equation} r_t = \\log (1 + R_t) = \\log \\frac{P_t}{P_{t-1}} = p_t - p_{t-1} \\end{equation}\n\\begin{equation} r_t(k) = r_t + r_{t-1} + \\dots + r_{t-k+1} \\end{equation}\nlog returns are similar for daily returns, less similar for yearly returns, and not necessarily similar for multi-year returns.\nRandom Walk Model In the random walk model, single-period log returns are assumed to be independent:\n\\begin{align} \\label{eq:rw} 1 + R_t(k) \u0026amp;= (1 + R_t)\\dots(1 + R_{t-k+1}) \\\\\\\n\u0026amp;= \\textrm{exp}(r_t) \\dots \\textrm{exp}(r_{t-k+1}) \\\\\\\n\u0026amp;= \\textrm{exp}(r_t + \\dots + r_{t-k+1}) \\end{align}\nIt is also sometimes assumed that log returns are \\(N(\\mu,\\sigma^2)\\) for some constant mean and variance. Then \\(k\\) period log returns are \\(N(k\\mu, k\\sigma^2)\\).\nGeometric Random Walks From eq:rw, we have that\n\\begin{equation} \\frac{P_t}{P_{t-k}} = 1 + R_t(k) = \\textrm{exp}(r_t + \\dots + r_{t-k+1}) \\end{equation}\nA process whose logarithm is a random walk is called a geometric random walk. If \\(r_1\\), \\(r_2\\), \\(\\dots\\) are i.i.d \\(N(\\mu, \\sigma^2)\\), then \\(P_t\\) is lognormal for all \\(t\\) and the process is a lognormal geometric random walk. \\(\\mu\\) is called the log-mean, and \\(\\sigma^2\\) the log-standard deviation of thet lognormal distribution of \\(exp(r_t)\\).\nValidity of the Random Walk Model In the lognormal geometric random walk model we assume that:\n log returns are normally distributed log returns are mutually independent  ", 
        "url": "https:\/\/braindump.jethro.dev\/posts\/statistical_methods_for_finance\/"
    },
    
    
    
    "https:\/\/braindump.jethro.dev\/posts\/statistical_testing\/": {
        
        "title": "Statistical Testing",

    "https:\/\/braindump.jethro.dev\/posts\/statistics\/": {
        
        "title": "Statistics",
        "tags": [],
        "content": "Basic Properties  \\(E(X) = \\sum x p(x)\\) \\(Var(X) = \\sum (x-\\mu)^2f(x)\\) X is around \\(E(X)\\), give or take \\(SD(X)\\) \\(E(aX + bY) = aE(X) + bE(Y)\\) \\(Var(aX + bY) = a^2Var(X) + b^2Var(Y)\\) \\(Var(X) = E(X^2) - [E(X)]^2\\) \\(Cov(X_1, X_2) = E(X_1X_2) - E(X_1)E(X_2)\\) if \\(X\\), \\(Y\\) are independent:  \\(M_{X+Y}(t) = M_X(t)M_Y(t)\\) \\(E(XY)=E(X)E(Y)\\), converse is true if \\(X\\) and \\(Y\\) are bivariate normal, extends to multivariate normal    Approximations Law of Large Numbers Let \\(X_1, X_2, \u0026hellip;, X_n\\) be IID, with expectation \\(\\mu\\) and variance \\(\\sigma^2\\). \\(\\overline{X_n} = \\frac{1}{n}\\sum^{n}_{i=1}X_i\\xrightarrow[n]{\\infty}\\mu\\). Let \\(x_1, x_2, \u0026hellip;, x_n\\) be realisations of the random variable \\(X_1, X_2, \u0026hellip;, X_n\\), then \\(\\overline{x_n} = \\frac{1}{n}\\sum^{n}_{i=1}x_n \\xrightarrow[n]{\\infty} \\mu\\)\nCentral Limit Theorem Let \\(S_n = \\sum^{n}_{i=1}X_i\\) where \\(X_1, X_2, \u0026hellip;, X_n\\) IID. \\(\\frac{S_n - n\\mu}{\\sqrt{n}\\sigma} \\xrightarrow[n]{\\infty} \\mathcal{N}(0,1)\\)\nDistributions Poisson(\\(\\lambda\\)) \\(E(X) = Var(X) = \\lambda\\)\nNormal \\(X \\sim \\mathcal{N}(\\mu, \\sigma^2)\\) https://kfrankc.com/posts/2018/10/19/normal-dist-derivation\n\\(f(x) = \\frac{1}{\\sqrt{2\\pi}\\sigma} exp \\left(-\\frac{(x-\\mu)^2}{2\\sigma^2}\\right), -\\infty\u0026lt;x\u0026lt;\\infty\\)\n When \\(\\mu = 0\\), \\(f(x)\\) is an even function, and \\(E(X^k) = 0\\) where \\(k\\) is odd \\(Y = \\frac{X-E(X)}{SD(X)}\\) is the standard normal  Gamma \\(\\Gamma\\) \\(g(t) = \\frac{\\lambda^\\alpha}{\\Gamma(\\alpha)}t^{\\alpha-1}e^{-\\lambda t}, t \\ge 0\\)\n\\(\\mu_1 = \\frac{\\alpha}{\\lambda}, \\mu_2 = \\frac{\\alpha(\\alpha+1)}{\\lambda^2}\\)\n\\(\\chi^2\\) Distribution Let \\(\\mathcal{Z} \\sim \\mathcal{N}(0,1)\\), \\(\\mathcal{U} = \\mathcal{Z}^2\\) has a \\(\\chi^2\\) distribution with 1 d.f.\n\\(f_{\\mathcal{U}}(u) = \\frac{1}{\\sqrt{2\\pi}} u^{-\\frac{1}{2}} e^{-\\frac{u}{2}}, u \\ge 0\\)\n\\(\\chi_1^2 \\sim \\Gamma(\\alpha=\\frac{1}{2}, \\lambda=\\frac{1}{2})\\)\nLet \\(U_1, U_2, \u0026hellip;, U_n\\) be \\(\\chi_1^2\\) IID, then \\(V=\\sum^{n}_{i=1}U_i\\) is \\(\\chi_n^2\\) with n degree freedom, \\(V \\sim \\Gamma(\\alpha=\\frac{n}{2}, \\lambda=\\frac{1}{2})\\)\n\\(E(\\chi_n^2) = n, Var(\\chi_n^2) = 2n\\)\n\\(M(t) = \\left(1 - 2t\\right)^{-\\frac{n}{2}}\\)\nt-distribution Let \\(\\mathcal{Z} \\sim \\mathcal{N}(0,1)\\), \\(\\mathcal{U}_n \\sim \\chi_n^2\\) be independent, \\(t_n = \\frac{\\mathcal{Z}}{\\sqrt{U_n / n}}\\) has a t-distribution with n d.f.\n\\(f(t) = \\frac{\\Gamma([(n+1)/2])}{\\sqrt{n}\\pi\\Gamma(n/2)}\\left(1 + \\frac{t^2}{n} \\right)^{-\\frac{n+1}{2}}\\)\n t is symmetric about 0 \\(t_n \\xrightarrow[n]{\\infty} \\mathcal{Z}\\)  F-distribution Let \\(U \\sim \\chi_m^2, V \\sim \\chi_n^2\\) be independent, \\(W = \\frac{U/m}{V/n}\\) has an F distribution with (m,n) d.f.\nIf \\(X \\sim t_n\\), \\(X^2 = \\frac{\\mathcal{Z}/1}{U_n/n}\\) is an F distribution with (1,n) d.f, with \\(w \\ge 0\\):\nFor \\(n \u0026gt; 2\\), \\(E(W) = \\frac{n}{n-2}\\)\nSampling Let \\(X_1, X_2, \u0026hellip;, X_n\\) be IID \\(\\mathcal{N}(\\mu, \\sigma^2)\\).\n\\(\\text{sample mean, } \\overline{X} = \\frac{1}{n}\\sum^{n}_{i=1}X_i\\)\n\\(\\text{sample variance, } S^2 = \\frac{1}{n-1}\\sum^{n}_{i=1}\\left(X_i-\\overline{X}\\right)^2\\)\nProperties of \\(\\overline{X}\\) and \\(S^2\\)  \\(\\overline{X}\\) and \\(S^2\\) are independent \\(\\overline{X} \\sim \\mathcal{N}(\\mu, \\frac{\\sigma^2}{n})\\) \\(\\frac{(n-1)S^2}{\\sigma^2} \\sim \\chi_{n-1}^2\\) \\(\\frac{\\overline{X} - \\mu}{S/\\sqrt{n}} \\sim t_{n-1}\\)  Simple Random Sampling (SRS) Assume \\(n\\) random draws are made without replacement. (Not SRS, will be corrected for later).\nSummary of Lemmas  \\(P(X_i =\\xi_j) = \\frac{n_j}{N}\\): Lemma A For \\(i \\ne j\\), \\(Cov(X_i, X_j) = - \\frac{\\sigma^2}{N-1}\\): Lemma B  Estimation Problem Let \\(X_1, X_2, \u0026hellip;, X_n\\) be random draws with replacement. Then \\(\\overline{X}\\) is an estimator of \\(\\mu\\). and the observed value of \\(\\overline{X}\\), \\(\\overline{x}\\) is an estimate of \\(\\mu\\).\nStandard Error (SE) SE of an \\(\\overline{X}\\) is defined to be \\(SD(\\overline{X})\\).\n   param est SE Est. SE     \\(\\mu\\) \\(\\overline{X}\\) \\(\\frac{\\sigma}{\\sqrt{n}}\\) \\(\\frac{s}{\\sqrt{n}}\\)   \\(p\\) \\(\\hat{p}\\) \\(\\sqrt{\\frac{p(1-p)}{n}}\\) \\(\\sqrt{\\frac{\\hat{p}(1-\\hat{p})}{n-1}}\\)    Without Replacement SE is multiplied by \\(\\frac{N-n}{N-1}\\), because \\(s^2\\) is biased for \\(\\sigma^2\\): \\(E(\\frac{N-1}{N}s^2) = \\sigma^2\\), but N is normally large.\nConfidence Interval An approximate \\(1-\\alpha\\) CI for \\(\\mu\\) is\n\\((\\overline{x} - z_{\\alpha/2}\\frac{s}{\\sqrt{n}}, \\overline{x} + z_{\\alpha/2}\\frac{s}{\\sqrt{n}})\\)\nBiased Measurements Let \\(X = \\mu + \\epsilon\\), where \\(E(\\epsilon) = 0\\), \\(Var(\\epsilon) = \\sigma^2\\)\nSuppose X is used to measure an unknown constant a, \\(a \\ne \\mu\\). \\(X = a + (\\mu - a) + \\epsilon\\), where \\(\\mu-a\\) is the bias.\nMean square error (MSE) is \\(E((X-a)^2) = \\sigma^2 + (\\mu - a)^2\\)\nwith n IID measurements, \\(\\overline{x} = \\mu + \\overline{\\epsilon}\\)\n\\(E((x - a)^2) = \\frac{\\sigma^2}{n} + \\left(\\mu - a\\right)^2\\)\n\\(\\text{MSE} = \\text{SE}^2 + \\text{bias}^2\\), hence \\(\\sqrt{\\text{MSE}}\\) is a good measure of the accuracy of the estimate \\(\\overline{x}\\) of a.\nEstimation of a Ratio Consider a population of \\(N\\) members, and two characteristics are recorded: \\((X_1, Y_1), (X_2, Y_2), \u0026hellip; , (X_n, Y_n)\\), \\(r = \\frac{\\mu_y}{\\mu_x}\\).\nAn obvious estimator of r is \\(R = \\frac{\\overline{Y}}{\\overline{X}}\\)\n\\(Cov(\\overline{X},\\overline{Y}) = \\frac{\\sigma_{xy}}{n}\\), where\n\\(\\sigma_{xy} := \\frac{1}{N}\\sum^{N}_{i=1}(x_i-\\mu_x)(x_i-\\mu_y)\\) is the population covariance.\nProperties \\(Var( R) \\approx \\frac{1}{\\mu_x^2}\\left(r^2\\sigma_{\\overline{X}}^2 + \\sigma_{\\overline{Y}}^2 - 2r\\sigma_{\\overline{X}\\overline{Y}}\\right)\\)\nPopulation coefficient \\(\\rho = \\frac{\\sigma_{xy}}{\\sigma_{x}\\sigma_{y}}\\)\n\\(E( R) \\approx r + \\frac{1}{n}\\left(\\frac{N-n}{N-1}\\right)\\frac{1}{\\mu_x^2}\\left(r\\sigma_x^2-\\rho\\sigma_x\\sigma_y\\right)\\)\n\\(s_{xy} = \\frac{1}{n-1}\\sum^{n}_{i=1}\\left(X_i - \\overline{X}\\right)\\left(Y_i - \\overline{Y}\\right)\\)\nRatio Estimates \\(\\overline{Y}_R = \\frac{\\mu_x}{\\overline{X}}\\overline{Y} = \\mu_xR\\)\n\\(Var(\\overline{Y}_R) \\approx \\frac{1}{n}\\frac{N-n}{N-1}(r^2\\sigma_x^2 + \\sigma_y^2 -2r\\rho\\sigma_x\\sigma_y)\\)\n\\(E(\\overline{Y}_R) - \\mu_y \\approx \\frac{1}{n}\\frac{N-n}{N-1}\\frac{1}{\\mu_x}\\left(r\\sigma_x^2 -\\rho\\sigma_x\\sigma_y\\right)\\)\nThe bias is of order \\(\\frac{1}{n}\\), small compared to its standard error.\n\\(\\overline{Y}_R\\) is better than \\(\\overline{Y}\\), having smaller variance, when \\(\\rho \u0026gt; \\frac{1}{2}\\left(\\frac{C_x}{C_y}\\right)\\), where \\(C_i = \\sigma_i/\\mu_i\\)\nVariance of \\(\\overline{Y}_R\\) can be estimated by\n\\(s_{\\overline{Y}_R}^2 = \\frac{1}{n}\\frac{N-n}{N-1}\\left(R^2s_x^2+s_y^2-2Rs_{xy}\\right)\\)\nAn approximate \\(1-\\alpha\\) C.I. for \\(\\mu_y\\) is \\(\\overline{Y}_R \\pm z_{\\alpha/2}s_{\\overline{Y}_R}\\)\nMethod of Moments To estimate \\(\\theta\\), express it as a function of moments \\(g(\\hat{\\mu}_1,\\hat{\\mu}_2,\u0026hellip;)\\)\nMonte Carlo Monte Carlo is used to generate many realisations of random variable.\n\\(\\overline{X} \\xrightarrow[n]{\\infty} \\alpha/\\lambda, \\hat{\\sigma}^2 \\xrightarrow[n]{\\infty}\\alpha/\\lambda^2\\), MOM estimators are consistent (asymptotically unbiased).\n\\(\\text{Poisson}(\\lambda)\\): \\(\\text{bias} = 0, SE \\approx \\sqrt{\\frac{\\overline{x}}{n}}\\)\n\\(N(\\mu, \\sigma^2)\\): \\(\\mu = \\mu_1\\), \\(\\sigma^2 = \\mu_2 - \\mu_1^2\\)\n\\(\\Gamma(\\lambda, \\alpha)\\): \\(\\hat{\\lambda} = \\frac{\\hat{\\mu}_1}{\\hat{\\mu}_2-\\hat{\\mu}_1^2}=\\frac{\\overline{X}}{\\hat{\\sigma}^2}, \\hat{\\alpha} = \\frac{\\hat{\\mu}_1^2}{\\hat{\\mu}_2-\\hat{\\mu}_1^2}=\\frac{\\overline{X}^2}{\\hat{\\sigma}^2}\\)\nMaximum Likelihood Estimator (MLE) Poisson Case \\(L(\\lambda) = \\prod^n_{i=1}\\frac{\\lambda^{x_i}e^{-\\lambda}}{x_i!} = \\frac{\\lambda\\sum^n_{i=1}x_ie^{-n\\lambda}}{\\prod^{n}_{i=1}x_i!}\\)\n\\(l(\\lambda) = \\sum^{n}_{i=1}x_i\\log\\lambda - n\\lambda - \\sum^{n}_{i=1}\\log x_i!\\)\nML estimate of \\(\\lambda_0\\) is \\(\\overline{x}\\). ML estimator is \\(\\hat{\\lambda}_0 = \\overline{X}\\)\nNormal case \\(l(\\mu, \\sigma) = -n\\log\\sigma - \\frac{n\\log 2\\pi}{2} - \\frac{\\sum^{n}_{i=1}\\left(X_i-\\mu\\right)^2}{2\\sigma^2}\\)\n\\(\\frac{\\partial l}{\\partial \\mu} = \\frac{\\sum \\left(X_i - \\mu\\right)}{\\sigma^2} \\implies \\hat{\\mu} = \\overline{x}\\)\n\\(\\frac{\\partial l}{\\partial \\sigma} = \\frac{\\sum^{n}_{i=1}\\left(X_i-\\mu\\right)^2}{\\sigma^3} - \\frac{n}{\\sigma} \\ \\implies \\hat{\\sigma^2} = \\frac{1}{n}\\sum^{n}_{i=1}\\left(X_i-\\overline{X}\\right)^2\\)\nGamma case \\(l(\\theta) = n\\alpha\\log\\lambda + (\\alpha -1)\\sum^{n}_{i=1}\\log X_i - \\lambda\\sum^{n}_{i=1} X_i - n\\log\\Gamma(\\alpha)\\)\n\\(\\frac{\\partial l}{\\partial \\alpha} = n\\log\\alpha + \\sum^{n}_{i=1}\\log X_i - \\sum^{n}_{i=1}X_i - \\frac{n}{\\Gamma(\\alpha)}\\Gamma \u0026lsquo;(\\alpha)\\)\n\\(\\frac{\\partial l}{\\partial \\lambda} = \\frac{n\\alpha}{\\lambda} - \\sum^{n}_{i=1}X_i\\)\n\\(\\hat{\\lambda} = \\frac{\\hat{\\alpha}}{\\hat{x}}\\)\nMultinomial Case \\(f(x_1, \u0026hellip;, x_r) = {n \\choose {x_1, x_2, \u0026hellip; x_r}} \\prod^{n}_{i=1} p_i^{X_i}\\)\nwhere \\(X_i\\) is the number of times the value occurs, and not the number of trials. and \\(x_1, x_2, \u0026hellip; x_r\\) are non-negative integers summing to \\(n\\). \\(\\forall i\\):\n\\(E(X_i) = np_i, Var(X_i)=np_i(1-p_i)\\)\n\\(Cov(X_i,X_j) = -np_ip_j, \\forall i \\ne j\\)\n\\(l(p) = \\Kappa + \\sum^{r-1}_{i=1}x_i\\log p_i + x_r\\log(1-p_1-\u0026hellip;-p_{r-1})\\)\n\\(\\frac{\\partial l}{\\partial p_i} = \\frac{x_i}{p_i} - \\frac{x_r}{p_r} = 0 \\text{ assuming MLE exists}\\)\n\\(\\frac{x_i}{\\hat{p}_i} = \\frac{x_r}{\\hat{p}_r} \\implies \\hat{p}_i = \\frac{x_i}{c}, c=\\frac{x_r}{\\hat{p}_r}\\)\n\\(\\sum^r_{i=1}\\hat{p}_i = \\sum^r_{i=1}\\frac{x_i}{c} = 1 \\ \\implies c = \\sum^{r}_{i=1}x_i = n \\implies \\hat{p}_i = \\frac{\\overline{x}_i}{n}\\)\nsame as MOM estimator.\nCIs in MLE \\(\\frac{\\hat{X} - \\mu}{s/\\sqrt{n}} \\sim t_{n-1}\\)\nGiven the realisations \\(\\overline{x}\\) and \\(s\\), \\(\\overline{x} \\pm t_{n-1, \\alpha/2}\\frac{s}{\\sqrt{n}},\\overline{x} + t_{n-1, \\alpha/2}\\frac{s}{\\sqrt{n}}\\) is the exact \\(1-\\alpha\\) CI for \\(\\mu\\).\n\\(\\frac{n\\hat{\\sigma}^2}{\\sigma^2} \\sim \\chi_{n-1}^\\), \\(\\frac{n\\hat{\\sigma}^2}{\\chi_{n-1,\\alpha/2}^2}, \\frac{n\\hat{\\sigma}^2}{\\chi_{n-1,1-\\alpha/2}^2}\\) is the exact \\(1-\\alpha\\) CI for \\(\\sigma\\).\nFisher Information \\(I\\left( \\theta \\right) = - E \\left( \\frac{\\partial}{\\partial \\theta^2} \\log f\\left( x | \\theta \\right) \\right)\\)\n   Distribution MLE Variance     Po(\\(\\lambda\\)) \\(X\\) \\(\\lambda\\)   Be(\\(p\\)) \\(X\\) \\(p\\left(1-p\\right)\\)   Bin(\\(n\\),\\(p\\)) \\(\\frac{X}{n}\\) \\(\\frac{p(1-p)}{n}\\)   HWE tri \\(\\frac{X_2+2X_3}{n}\\) \\(\\frac{\\theta(1-\\theta)}{n}\\)    General trinomial: \\(\\left(\\frac{X_1}{n}, \\frac{X_2}{n} \\right)\\)\n\\begin{equation*} \\begin{bmatrix} p_1(1-p_1) \u0026amp; -p_1p_2 \\ -p_1p_2 \u0026amp; p_2(1-p_2) \\end{bmatrix} \\frac{1}{n} \\end{equation*}\nIn all the above cases, \\(\\text{var}(\\hat{\\theta}) = I(\\theta)^{-1}\\).\nAsymptotic Normality of MLE As \\(n \\rightarrow \\infty\\), \\(\\sqrt{nI(\\theta)}(\\hat{\\theta} - \\theta) \\rightarrow N(0,1)\\) in distribution, and hence \\(\\hat{\\theta} \\sim N\\left(\\theta, \\frac{I\\left( \\theta \\right)^{-1}}{n}\\right)\\)\nAs \\(\\hat{\\theta} \\xrightarrow[n]{\\infty} \\theta\\), MLE is consistent.\nSE of an estimate of \\(\\theta\\) is the SD of the estimator \\(\\hat{\\theta}\\), hence \\(SE = SD(\\hat{\\theta}) = \\sqrt{\\frac{I(\\theta)^{-1}}{n}} \\approx \\sqrt{\\frac{I(\\hat{\\theta})^{-1}}{n}}\\)\n\\(1-\\alpha \\text{ CI } \\approx \\hat{\\theta} \\pm z_{\\alpha/2}\\sqrt{\\frac{I(\\theta)^{-1}}{n}}\\)\nEfficiency Cramer-Rao Inequality: if \\(\\theta\\) is unbiased, then \\(\\forall \\theta \\in \\Theta\\) , \\(var(\\hat{\\theta}) \\ge I(\\hat{\\theta})^{-1}/n\\), if = then \\(\\hat{\\theta}\\) is efficient.\n\\(eff(\\hat{\\theta}) = \\frac{I(\\hat{\\theta})^{-1}/n}{var(\\hat{\\theta})}\u0026lt; 1\\)\nSufficiency Characterisation Let \\(S_t = {x: T(x) = t}\\). The sample space of \\(X\\), \\(S\\) is the disjoint union of \\(S_t\\) across all possible values of \\(T\\).\n\\(T\\) is sufficient for \\(\\theta\\) if \\(\\exists q() \\text{ s.t. } \\forall x \\in S_t, f_{\\theta}(X\\x|T=t) = q(x)\\).\nFactorisation Theorem \\(T\\) is sufficient for \\(\\theta\\) iff \\(\\exists g(t,\\theta), h(x) \\text{ s.t. } \\forall \\theta \\in \\Theta, f_\\theta(x) = g(T(x), \\theta) h(x) \\forall x\\)\nRao-Blackwell Theorem Let \\(\\hat{\\theta}\\) be an estimator of \\(\\theta\\) with finite variance, \\(T\\) be sufficient for \\(\\theta\\). Let \\(\\tilde{\\theta} = E[\\hat{\\theta}|T]\\). Then for every \\(\\theta \\in \\Theta\\), \\(E\\left(\\hat{\\theta} - \\theta\\right)^2 \\le E\\left(\\hat{\\theta}-\\theta\\right)^2\\). Equality holds iff \\(\\hat{\\theta}\\) is a function of \\(T\\).\nRandom Conditional Expectation  \\(E(X) = E(E(X|T))\\) \\(var(X) = var(E(X|T)) + E(var(X|T))\\) \\(var(Y|X) =E(Y^2|X) - E(Y|X)^2\\) \\(E(Y) = Y, var(Y) =0\\) iff \\(Y\\) is a constant  Hypothesis Testing Let \\(X_1\u0026hellip; X_n\\) be IID with density \\(f(x|\\theta)\\). null \\(H_0: \\theta = \\theta_0\\), \\(H-1 : \\theta = \\theta_1\\). Critical region is \\(R\\subset R_n\\). \\(size = P_0(X \\in R)\\) and \\(power = P_1(X\\in R)\\).\n\\(\\Lambda(x) = \\frac{f_0(x_1)\u0026hellip;f_0(x_n)}{f_1(x_1)\u0026hellip;f_1(x_n)}\\). Critical region \\({x : \\Lambda(x) \u0026lt; c_\\alpha}\\), and among all tests with this size, it has the maximum power (Neyman-Pearson Lemma).\nA hypothesis is simple if it completely specifies the distibution of the data.\n\\(H_1 : \\mu \u0026gt; \\mu_0\\): Critical region \\(\\{\\bar{x} \u0026gt; \\mu_0 + z_\\alpha\\frac{\\sigma}{\\sqrt{n}}\\}\\), the power is a function of \\(\\mu\\), and this is uniformly the most powerful test for size \\(\\le \\alpha\\).\n\\(H_1 : \\mu \\ne \\mu_0\\): Critical region \\(\\{|\\bar{x}-\\mu_0| \u0026gt; c\\}, c = z_{\\frac{\\alpha}{2}}\\frac{\\sigma}{\\sqrt{n}}\\), but not uniformly most powerful.\nThe \\((1-\\alpha)\\) CI for \\(\\mu\\) consists of precisely the values \\(\\mu_0\\) for which \\(H_0: \\mu = \\mu_0\\) is not rejected against \\(H_1: \\mu \\ne \\mu_0\\). Exact for normal with known variance, approx. in others.\np-value the probability under \\(H_0\\) that the test statistic is more extreme than the realisation. (A, B): \\(p = p_0(\\bar{X} \u0026gt; \\bar{x}) = P(Z\u0026gt;\\frac{\\bar{x} - \\mu_0}{\\sigma/\\sqrt{n}})\\). (C): \\(p = P_0(|\\bar{X} - \\mu_0| \u0026gt; |\\bar{x} - \\mu_0|)\\). The smaller the p-value, the more suspicious one should be about \\(H_0\\). If size is smaller than p-value, do not reject \\(H_0\\).\nGeneralized Likelihood Ratio \\(\\Lambda^* = \\frac{\\text{max}_{\\theta \\in \\omega_0}L(\\theta)}{\\text{max}_{\\theta\\in\\Omega}L(\\theta)}\\), \\(\\Omega = \\omega_0 \\cup \\omega_1\\). The closer \\(\\Lambda\\) is to 0, the stronger the evidence for \\(H_1\\).\nLarge-sample null distribution of \\(\\Lambda\\) Under \\(H_0\\), when n is large, \\(-2\\log\\Lambda = \\chi_k^2\\), where \\(k = \\text{dim}(\\Omega) - \\text{dim}(\\omega_0)\\).\nNormal (C): \\(p = P\\left(\\chi_1^2 \u0026gt; \\frac{(\\bar{x} - \\mu_0)^2}{\\sigma^2/n}\\right)\\)\nMultinomial: \\(\\Lambda = \\prod_{i=1}^{r} \\left(\\frac{E_i}{X_i}\\right)^{X_i}\\) where \\(E_i = np_i(\\hat{\\theta})\\) is the expected frequency of the ith event under \\(H_0\\). \\(-2\\log\\Lambda \\approx \\sum_{i=1}^{r}\\frac{(X_i-E_i)^2}{E_i}\\), which is the Pearson chi-square statistic, written as \\(X^2\\).\nPoisson Dispersion Test For \\(i = 1 \u0026hellip; n\\) let \\(X_i \\sim Poisson(\\lambda_i)\\) are independent.\n\\(w_0 = \\{ \\tilde{\\lambda} | \\lambda_1 = \\lambda_2 = \u0026hellip; = \\lambda_n\\}\\)\n\\(w_1 = \\{\\tilde{\\lambda} | \\lambda_i \\ne \\lambda_j \\text{ for some } i,j\\}\\)\n\\(-2\\log\\Lambda \\approx \\frac{\\sum_{i=1}^{n}(X_i-\\bar{X})^2}{\\bar{X}}\\). For large n, the null distribution of \\(-2\\log\\Lambda\\) is approximately \\(\\chi_{n-1}^2\\)\nComparing 2 samples Normal Theory: Same Variance \\(X_1, \u0026hellip;, X_n\\) be i.i.d \\(N(\\mu_X,\\sigma^2)\\) and \\(Y_1,\u0026hellip;,Y_m\\) be i.i.d \\(N(\\mu_Y, \\sigma^2)\\), independent. \\(H_0: \\mu_X - \\mu_Y = d\\)\nKnown Variance \\(Z := \\frac{\\bar{X} - \\bar{Y} - (\\mu_X - \\mu_Y)}{\\sigma{\\sqrt{\\frac{1}{n} + \\frac{1}{m}}}}\\) and reject \\(H_0\\) when \\(|Z| \u0026gt; z_{\\alpha/2}\\)\nUnknown Variance \\(s_p^2 = \\frac{(n-1)s_X^2 + (m-1)s_Y^2}{m+n-2}\\) where \\(s_X^2 = \\frac{1}{n-1}\\sum_{i=1}^{n}(X_i-\\bar{X})^2\\). \\(s_p^2\\) is an unbiased estimator of \\(\\sigma^2\\). \\(s_X\\) within factor of 2 from \\(s_Y\\).\n\\(t := \\frac{\\bar{X} - \\bar{Y} - (\\mu_X - \\mu_Y)}{s_p{\\sqrt{\\frac{1}{n} + \\frac{1}{m}}}}\\) follows a t distribution with \\(m+n-2\\) d.f.\nIf two-sided: reject \\(H_0\\) when \\(|t| \u0026gt; t_{n+m-2,\\alpha/2}\\). If one-sided, e.g \\(H_1: \\mu_X \u0026gt; \\mu_Y\\), reject \\(H_0\\) when \\(t \u0026gt; t_{n+m-2,\\alpha}\\).\nCI \\(\\frac{\\bar{X}-\\bar{Y}}\\pm z_{\\alpha/2} \\cdot \\sigma \\sqrt{\\frac{1}{n} + \\frac{1}{m}}\\) if \\(\\sigma\\) is known, or \\(\\frac{\\bar{X}-\\bar{Y}}\\pm t_{m+n-2, \\alpha/2} \\cdot s_p \\sqrt{\\frac{1}{n} + \\frac{1}{m}}\\) if \\(\\sigma\\) is unknown.\nUnequal Variance \\(Z := \\frac{\\bar{X} - \\bar{Y} - (\\mu_X - \\mu_Y)}{{\\sqrt{\\frac{\\sigma_X^2}{n} + \\frac{\\sigma_Y^2}{m}}}}\\)\n\\(t := \\frac{\\bar{X} - \\bar{Y} - (\\mu_X - \\mu_Y)}{{\\sqrt{\\frac{s_X^2}{n} + \\frac{s_Y^2}{m}}}}\\), with \\(df = \\frac{(a+b)^2}{\\frac{a^2}{n-1} + \\frac{b^2}{m-1}}\\) where \\(a = \\frac{s_X^2}{n}\\) and \\(b = \\frac{s_Y^2}{m}\\)\nMann-Whitney Test We take the smaller sample of size \\(n_1\\), and sum the ranks in that sample. \\(R\u0026rsquo; = n_1(m+n+1) -R\\), and \\(R* = min(R\u0026rsquo;,R)\\), we reject \\(H_0: F = G\\) if \\(R*\\) is too small.\nTest works for all distributions, and is robust to outliers.\nPaired Samples \\((X_i, Y_i)\\) are paired and related to the same individual. \\((X_i, Y_i)\\) is independent from \\((X_j, Y_j)\\). Compute \\(D_i = Y_i - X_i\\), To test \\(H_0 : \\mu_D = d\\), \\(t = \\frac{\\bar{D} - \\mu_D}{s_D/\\sqrt{n}}\\).\n\\(1-\\alpha\\) CI: \\(\\bar{D}\\pm t_{n-1,\\alpha/2}S_D/\\sqrt{n}\\)\nRanked Test \\(W_+\\) is the sum of ranks among all positive \\(D_i\\) and \\(W_i\\) is the sum of ranks among all negative \\(D_i\\). We want to reject \\(H_0\\) if \\(W = min(W_+, W_-)\\) is too large.\n",

    "https:\/\/braindump.jethro.dev\/posts\/stochastic_processes\/": {
        
        "title": "Stochastic Processes",
        "tags": [],
        "content": "A stochastic process \\(X(t), t \\in T\\) is a collection of random variables. For each \\(t \\in T\\), \\(X(t)\\) is a random variable. The index \\(t\\) is often interpreted as time, and as a result, we refer to \\(X(t)\\) as the state of the process at time \\(t\\).\nThe set \\(T\\) is called the index set of the process. When \\(T\\) is a countable set, the stochastic is a discrete-time process. If \\(T\\) is an interval of the real line, the process is said to be a continuous-time process.\nFocus: Discrete time, discrete state space Markov Chain\n  Stochastic = random\n  A stochastic process describes random phenomena that change over time\n  values that \\(X_t\\)\u0026lsquo;s take\n  set of all possible states, denoted by \\(\\mathcal{S}\\).\n  can be thought of as time. If \\(T = \\{0, 1, 2, \\dots \\}\\) then it is a discrete-time process. If \\(T\\) is an interval, it is a continuous time process.\n  Each \\(X_t\\) is a random variable.\nExample of stochastic process: Gambler\u0026rsquo;s ruin\n A gambler starts with an initial fortune of \\(k\\) dollars. The gambler plays against \\(B\\) with an initial fortune of \\(N-k\\) dollars. Each game he bets $1, wins with probability \\(p\\) Let \\(\\{X_t = t = 0,1,2 \\dots\\}\\) represent his fortune as the betting goes on. Game only stops when either gambler or \\(B\\) is ruined. Here \\(\\mathcal{S} = \\{0,1,\\dots,N\\}\\) For a realization of the results of the first 10 games, (here \\(p=1/2\\)):   sample(c(-1, 1), 10, replace=T) Reference Textbooks , Ross, n.d., @pinsky2010introduction\n", 
        "url": "https:\/\/braindump.jethro.dev\/posts\/stochastic_processes\/"
    },

    "https:\/\/braindump.jethro.dev\/posts\/studying\/": {
        
        "title": "Studying",
        "tags": [],
        "content": "The optimal studying strategy  Glance at past year paper questions  Rationale: understand the kind of questions to be asked, prime the brain for chunking, mental pump\n Glance through relevant textbook chapters  Same rationale. Understand the big picture.\nfor n from 1..N\n Glance at tutorial n  Understand the problems that typically come out from this chapter.\n Glance at textbook chapter subheadings/structure.  Form big picture.\n Read textbook with 2 passes minimum.  First pass to get general feel, less math Second pass to understand deeper    Work through the example problems at least once after first pass, when encountered during second pass. Attempt to recall during break, in a different location. Try to solve the problem without referencing solution.\nRework this problem after the break.\n", 
        "url": "https:\/\/braindump.jethro.dev\/posts\/studying\/"
    },
    
    
    
    "https:\/\/braindump.jethro.dev\/posts\/sufficient_statistics\/": {
        
        "title": "Sufficient Statistics",
        "tags": [],
        "content": "A statistic \\(t\\) is called a sufficient statistic for \\(\\theta\\) for a given \\(\\boldsymbol{y}\\) if:\n\\begin{equation} p(\\boldsymbol{y} | t, \\theta)=p(\\boldsymbol{y} | t) \\end{equation}\nLet \\(Y_{i} \\sim \\text { Bernoulli }(\\theta)\\) for \\(i = 1, \\dots, n\\), and \\(T=\\sum_{i=1}^{n} Y_{i}\\). Then it can be shown that \\(t=\\sum_{i=1}^{n} y_{i}\\) is a sufficient statistic for \\(\\theta\\) given \\(y=\\left(y_{1}, \\ldots, y_{n}\\right)\\).\nFisher-Neyman Theorem The Fisher-Neyman theorem, or the factorization theorem, helps us find sufficient statistics more readily. It states that:\nA statistic \\(t\\) is sufficient for \\(\\theta\\) if and only if there are functions \\(f\\) and \\(g\\) such that:\n\\begin{equation} p(\\boldsymbol{y} | \\theta)=f(t, \\theta) g(\\boldsymbol{y}) \\end{equation}\nwhere \\(t=t(\\boldsymbol{y})\\).\n", 
        "url": "https:\/\/braindump.jethro.dev\/posts\/sufficient_statistics\/"
    },

    "https:\/\/braindump.jethro.dev\/posts\/neftci2019_surrogate_gradient_learning_snn\/": {
        
        "title": "Surrogate Gradient Learning In Spiking Neural Networks",
        "tags": [],
        "content": " paper https://arxiv.org/pdf/1901.09948v2.pdf  Spiking Neural Networks enable power-efficient network models, which have become of increasing importance in embedded and auto-motive applications. The power efficiency stems from dispensing of expensive floating-point computations.\nSurrogate gradient methods overcome the difficulties associated with the discontinuous non-linearity. Rather than changing the neuronal model (Smoothed Spiking Neural Networks), surrogate gradients are introduced to allow for numerical optimisation.\nSurrogate gradients can also improve the memory access overhead of the learning process. For example, a global loss can be replaced by a number of local loss functions. Surrogate gradient methods also allow for end-to-end training without specifying a coding scheme in the hidden layers.\nThere are many different available surrogate functions used, and all have reportedly some success Neftci, Mostafa, and Zenke, n.d.. All of the functions used are non-linear and monotonically increasing towards the firing threshold. This suggests that the details of the surrogate are not crucial in ensuring success of the method.\n", 
        "url": "https:\/\/braindump.jethro.dev\/posts\/neftci2019_surrogate_gradient_learning_snn\/"
    },

    "https:\/\/braindump.jethro.dev\/posts\/synaptic_current_model\/": {
        
        "title": "Synaptic Current Model",
        "tags": [],
        "content": "Synaptic currents are generated by synaptic currents triggered by arrival of presynaptic spikes \\(S_{j}^{(l)}(t)\\). Spike trains \\(S_{j}^{(l)}(t)\\) are denoted as a sum of Dirac delta functions \\(S_{j}^{(l)}(t)=\\sum_{s \\in C_{j}^{(l)}} \\delta(t-s)\\), where \\(s\\) runs over the firing times \\(C_j^{(l)}\\) of neuron \\(j\\) in layer \\(l\\).\nA good first-order approximation of the synaptic current is one of exponential decay. Synaptic currents are also assumed to sum linearly.\n\\begin{equation} \\label{eq:scm} \\frac{\\mathrm{d} I_{i}^{(l)}}{\\mathrm{d} t}=-\\underbrace{\\frac{I_{i}^{(l)}(t)}{\\tau_{\\mathrm{syn}}}}_{\\mathrm{exp} . \\text { decay }}+\\underbrace{\\sum_{j} W_{i j}^{(l)} S_{j}^{(l-1)}(t)}_{\\text {feed-forward }}+\\underbrace{\\sum_{j} V_{i j}^{(l)} S_{j}^{(l)}(t)}_{\\text {recurrent }} \\end{equation}\nA single LIF neuron can be simulated with 2 linear differential equations whose initial conditions change instantaneously when a spike occurs. Combining the reset term with the equation for the Leaky Integrate-And-Fire model, we get:\n\\begin{equation} \\label{eq:lif_with_reset} \\frac{\\mathrm{d} U_{i}^{(l)}}{\\mathrm{d} t}=-\\frac{1}{\\tau_{\\mathrm{mem}}}\\left(\\left(U_{i}^{(l)}-U_{\\mathrm{rest}}\\right)+R I_{i}^{(l)}\\right)+S_{i}^{(l)}(t)\\left(U_{\\mathrm{rest}}-\\vartheta\\right) \\end{equation}\nThe solutions to Equations eq:scm and eq:lif_with_reset are approximated numerically by discretizing time, and expressing the output spike-train \\(S_{i}^{(l)}(n)\\) of neuron \\(i\\) in layer \\(l\\) at time-step \\(n\\) as a non-linear function of the membrane voltage \\(S_i^{(l)}(n) \\equiv \\Theta(U_i^{(l)(n)} - \\theta)\\) where \\(\\theta\\) is the Heaviside step function, and \\(\\theta\\) is the firing threshold.\nSetting \\(U_{\\text{rest}} = 0\\), \\(R=1\\), \\(\\theta=1\\), and using some small simulation time step \\(\\delta t \u0026gt; 0\\), we get:\n\\begin{equation} I_{i}^{(l)}[n+1]=\\alpha I_{i}^{(l)}[n]+\\sum_{j} W_{i j}^{(l)} S_{j}^{(l)}[n]+\\sum_{j} V_{i j}^{(l)} S_{j}^{(l)}[n] \\end{equation}\nwith decay strength \\(\\alpha \\equiv \\exp \\left(-\\frac{\\Delta_{t}}{\\tau_{\\mathrm{syn}}}\\right)\\). Equation eq:lif_with_reset can then be expressed as:\n\\begin{equation} U_{i}^{(l)}[n+1]=\\beta U_{i}^{(l)}[n]+I_{i}^{(l)}[n]-S_{i}^{(l)}[n] \\end{equation}\nwith \\(\\beta \\equiv \\exp \\left(-\\frac{\\Delta_{t}}{\\tau_{\\operatorname{mem}}}\\right)\\). These two equations characterise the dynamics of a RNN. Specifically, the state of neuron \\(i\\) is given by the instantaneous synaptic currents \\(I_i\\) and the membrane voltage \\(U_i\\).\nReferences  (Neftci, Mostafa, and Zenke, n.d.)  Bibliography Neftci, Emre O., Hesham Mostafa, and Friedemann Zenke. n.d. “Surrogate Gradient Learning in Spiking Neural Networks.” http://arxiv.org/abs/1901.09948v2.\n", 
        "url": "https:\/\/braindump.jethro.dev\/posts\/synaptic_current_model\/"
    },
    
    
    
    "https:\/\/braindump.jethro.dev\/posts\/syntopic_reading\/": {
        
        "title": "Syntopic Reading",
        "tags": [],
        "content": "Syntopic reading is reading to build up an understanding on an entire literature.\nMichael Nielsen 1 does syntopic reading by doing the following steps:\n Engaging deeply in an important paper in the literature (e.g. AlphaGo paper). Engaging deeply in around 5-10 other key papers in the field, and shallow reads of other papers in the field.  This is because it is hard to identify what the most important papers are, and shallow reads help identify them.   Identify open problems and questions to ask. Identify tricks, and observations. Identify field-wide blind spots.  Michael Nielsen uses spaced repetition, adding Anki cards for each of the items in 3-5.\n  Augmenting Long-term Memory \u0026#x21a9;\u0026#xfe0e;\n   ", 
        "url": "https:\/\/braindump.jethro.dev\/posts\/syntopic_reading\/"
    },
    
    
    
    "https:\/\/braindump.jethro.dev\/posts\/system_design\/": {
        
        "title": "System Design",
        "tags": [],
        "content": " tags Software Engineering, Coding Interview Cheatsheet  Basics  SQL, noSQL Concurrency  Threads, deadlock, starvation read/write locks   Networking  Routers/Switches TCP vs UDP   File Systems  OS, file system, database levels of caching in modern OS    Terminologies Replication Replication refers to frequently copying the data across multiple machines. Post replication, multiple copies of the data exists across machines. This might help in case one or more of the machines die due to some failure.\nConsistency Assuming you have a storage system which has more than one machine, consistency implies that the data is same across the cluster, so you can read or write to/from any node and get the same data. Eventual consistency : Exactly what the name suggests. In a cluster, if multiple machines store the same data, an eventual consistent model implies that all machines will have the same data eventually. Its possible that at a given instance, those machines have different versions of the same data ( temporarily inconsistent ) but they will eventually reach a state where they have the same data.\nAvailability In the context of a database cluster, Availability refers to the ability to always respond to queries ( read or write ) irrespective of nodes going down.\nPartition Tolerance In the context of a database cluster, cluster continues to function even if there is a “partition” (communications break) between two nodes (both nodes are up, but can’t communicate).\nVertical scaling and Horizontal scaling In simple terms, to scale horizontally is adding more servers. To scale vertically is to increase the resources of the server ( RAM, CPU, storage, etc. ). Example: Lets say you own a restaurant which is now exceeding its seating capacity. One way of accomodating more people ( scaling ) would be to add more and more chairs (scaling vertically). However since the space is limited, you won’t be able to add more chairs once the space is full. Another way of scaling would be to open new branches of the restaurant ( horizontal scaling ). Source : http://stackoverflow.com/questions/5401992/what-does-scale-horizontally-and-scale-vertically-mean\nSharding With most huge systems, data does not fit on a single machine. In such cases, sharding refers to splitting the very large database into smaller, faster and more manageable parts called data shards.\nCAP Theorem It is impossible to simultaneously guarantee the following:\n Consistency Availability Partition Tolerance  Approaching System Design Questions  Feature Clarification (2 minutes) Estimations  Does the data fit in one machine/database? Can the cache fit on one machine/database?   Design Goals  Can data loss be tolerated?   Skeleton of design  Discuss high-level components; go into deep dive only on request    Caching Types  Write-through - write to both cache and db at the same time, before confirming write completion  Write latency is higher but re-reading writes and reads is fast   Write-around - write to db, missing cache  cache must fetch reads from db on first try higher read latency   Write-back - I/O completion sent when data written to cache  cache writes to db might lose data allieviated with replicates    Implementing LRU caching Hashtable + Doubly-linked list Key -\u0026gt; Pointer to node in doubly linked list Each time it is accessed, move node to head of doubly-linked list Evicting keys: If adding new item, and it is full, remove tail of list,\nSAS/SSD  used for I/O over SATA (7.5krpm)  Implementing TinyURL Feature Clarification  Shorten a URL Expand a slug into a URL Allow users to pick a custom URL  Data Estimation  Assume tinyURL load, 100M new writes per month Then, in 5 years, 6B writes. To handle 6B slugs, assuming we\u0026rsquo;re using [A-z][a-z][0-9] 62^k \u0026gt; 6*10^9 slugs need just 6 characters, 6 bytes.  Slugs will take up 36GB.   Assume 500 bytes for a URL, URLs will take up 3TB.  It is reasonable to store all of this on a single machine. But large amounts of reads and writes going to one machine can cause deadlock Master-slave replication    Design Goals    Latency Consistency Availability     Yes Yes C \u0026gt; A    Design API  shortenURL(url) expandURL(hash)  Computing the Hash convert_to_base_62(md5(url + salt))[:6]\nStateless application servers load balancers ensure application is available when a server dies, and client knows which server to talk to\nImplementing Search Implementing a distributed key-store  Data can\u0026rsquo;t fit onto one machine So now the choice is between consistency and availability Perform some estimations  ", 
        "url": "https:\/\/braindump.jethro.dev\/posts\/system_design\/"
    },
    
    
    
    "https:\/\/braindump.jethro.dev\/posts\/systems_programming\/": {
        
        "title": "Systems Programming",
        "tags": [],
        "content": " tags Operating Systems, Linux  Memory TODO: https://people.freebsd.org/~lstewart/articles/cpumemory.pdf\nDrepper, n.d.\n\u0026lt;biblio.bib\u0026gt;\n", 
        "url": "https:\/\/braindump.jethro.dev\/posts\/systems_programming\/"

    "https:\/\/braindump.jethro.dev\/posts\/t_distribution\/": {
        
        "title": "t-distribution",
        "tags": [],
        "content": "The Student\u0026rsquo;s t-distribution arises when estimating the mean of a normally distributed population, when the sample size is small and the population standard deviation is unknown.\nThe Student\u0026rsquo;s t-test assess the statistical significance of the difference between 2 sample means. It also arises in Bayesian analyses of data from a normal family.\n", 
        "url": "https:\/\/braindump.jethro.dev\/posts\/t_distribution\/"
    },

        "title": "Talks: Emacs Lisp Development Tips with John Wiegley",
        "tags": [],
        "content": "Interactive debugging  In debug buffer: e to evaluate any lisp expression in stack-frame use (debug) to enter debugger eldoc-mode to show operation  Profiling  elp-instrument-function elp-instrument-package  Speeding up Functions  mutation in place:  nconc nreverse setcar setcdr    ",

    "https:\/\/braindump.jethro.dev\/posts\/technical_analyses\/": {
        
        "title": "Technical Analyses",
        "tags": [],
        "content": "The three tenets of technical analyses are:\nMarket action discounts everything Anything that can possibly affect the price is reflected in the price of the market. Therefore all that is required is the study of price action.\nPrices move in trends This is the market equivalent of Newton\u0026rsquo;s first law: a trend in motion is more likely to continue than to reverse.\nHistory repeats itself Patterns that worked well in the past will continue to work well in the future.\n", 
        "url": "https:\/\/braindump.jethro.dev\/posts\/technical_analyses\/"
    },
    
    
    
    "https:\/\/braindump.jethro.dev\/posts\/comsa2019_temp_coding\/": {
        
        "title": "Temp Coding with Alpha Synaptic Function",
        "tags": [],
        "content": " tags Spiking Neural Networks  Temporal Coding with Alpha Synaptic Function (Comsa et al., n.d.) Motivation  Atemporal networks (think LSTMs) don\u0026rsquo;t have the benefits of encoding information directly in the temporal domain  They remain sequential (require all previous layers of computation to produce answer) Information in the real world are typically temporal    Key Ideas   Temporal Coding: Information is encoded in the relative timing of neuron spikes. Using temporal coding allows shift of differentiable relationship into the temporal domain.\n Find differentiable relationship of the time of postsynaptic spike with respect to the weights and times of the presynaptic spikes.    Alpha synaptic transfer function: Use the SRM, but with the exponential decay of form \\(t e^{-t}\\).\n  Synchronization pulses: input-independent spikes, used to facilitate transformations of the class boundaries.\n  The Coding Scheme More salient information about a feature is encode as an earlier spike in the corresponding input neuron (think time-to-first-spike).\nIn a classification problem with \\(m\\) inputs and \\(n\\) possible classes:\n input spike times of \\(m\\) input neurons output index of output neuron that fires first (among the \\(n\\) output neurons)  Alpha Synaptic Function Incoming exponential synaptic kernels are of the form \\(\\epsilon(t) = \\tau^{-1}e^{-\\tau t}\\) for some decay constant \\(\\tau\\). Potential of membrane in response to the spike is then \\(u(t) = t e^{-\\tau t}\\). It has a gradual rise, and slow decay.\n  Figure 1: Plot of \\(y = x e^{-10x}, x \\in [0, 1]\\)\n  Modelling Membrane Potential The membrane potential is a weighted sum of the presynaptic inputs:\n\\begin{equation} V_{mem}(t) = \\sum_{i} w_i (t-t_i)e^{\\tau(t_i - t)} \\end{equation}\nWe can compute the spike time \\(t_{out}\\) of a neuron by considering the minimal subset of presynaptic inputs \\(I_{t_{out}}\\) with \\(t_i \\le t_{out}\\) such that:\n\\begin{equation} \\label{eqn:threshold} \\sum_{i \\in {I_{t_{out}}}} w_i \\left( t_{out} - t_{i} \\right) e^{\\tau (t_i - t_{out})} = \\theta \\end{equation}\n has 2 solutions: 1 on rising part of function and another on decaying part. The spike time is the earlier solution.\nSolving for the Equation  Let \\(A_{I} = \\sum_{i \\in I} w_i e^{\\tau t_i}\\), and \\(B_{I} = \\sum_{i \\in I} w_i e^{\\tau t_i} t_i\\), we can compute:\n\\begin{equation} t_{out} = \\frac{B_I}{A_I} - \\frac{1}{\\tau}W\\left( -\\tau \\frac{\\theta}{A_I}e^{\\tau \\frac{B_I}{A_I}} \\right) \\end{equation}\nwhere \\(W\\) is the Lambert W function.\nThe Loss Function The loss minimizes the spike time of the target neuron, and maximizes the spike time of non-target neurons (cross-entropy!)\nSoftmax on the negative values of the spike times \\(o_{i}\\) (which are always positive):\n\\begin{equation} p_j = \\frac{e^{- o_j}}{\\sum_{i=1}^{n} e^{- o_i}} \\end{equation}\nThe cross entropy loss \\(L(y_i, p_i) = - \\sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i \\ln p_i\\) is used.\nChanging the weights of the network alters the spike times. We can compute the exact derivative of the post synaptic spike time wrt any presynaptic spike time \\(t_j\\) and its weight \\(w_j\\) as:\n\\begin{equation} \\frac{\\partial t_{out}}{\\partial t_j} = \\frac{w_j e^{t_j} \\left( t_j - \\frac{B_I}{A_I} + W_I + 1\\right)}{A_I (1 + W_I)} \\end{equation}\n\\begin{equation} \\frac{\\partial t_{out}}{\\partial w_j} = \\frac{e^{t_j} \\left( t_j - \\frac{B_I}{A_I} + W_I + 1\\right)}{A_I (1 + W_I)} \\end{equation}\nwhere\n\\begin{equation} W_I = W\\left( -\\frac{\\theta}{A_I}e^{\\frac{B_I}{A_I}} \\right) \\end{equation}\nSynchronization Pulses These act as a temporal form of bias, adjusting class boundaries in the temporal domain. Per network, or per layer biases are added. Spike times for each pulse are learned with the rest of the parameters of the network.\nHyperparameters   Experiments Boolean Logic Problems Inputs encoded as individual spike times of two input neurons. All spikes occur between 0 and 1. True and False values are drawn from distributions \\([0.0, 0.45]\\) and \\([0.55, 1.0]\\) respectively.\nTrained for maximum of 100 epochs, 1000 training examples. Tested on 150 randomly generated test examples. 100% accuracy on all problems.\nNon-convolutional MNIST 784 neurons of the input layer corresponding to pixels of the image. Darker pixels encoded as earlier spike times. Output of network is the index of the earliest neuron to spike.\nTrained with evolutionary-neural hybrid agents. Best networks achieved 99.96% and 97.96% accuracy on train and test sets.\nThe network learns two operating modes: slow-regime and fast-regime. Operating in the slow regime has higher accuracy, but takes more time. Fast regime makes quick decisions, with the first spike in the output layer occurring before the mean spike in the hidden layer.\n  Bibliography Comsa, Iulia M., Krzysztof Potempa, Luca Versari, Thomas Fischbacher, Andrea Gesmundo, and Jyrki Alakuijala. n.d. “Temporal Coding in Spiking Neural Networks with Alpha Synaptic Function.” http://arxiv.org/abs/1907.13223v1.\n", 
        "url": "https:\/\/braindump.jethro.dev\/posts\/comsa2019_temp_coding\/"
    },
    
    
    
    "https:\/\/braindump.jethro.dev\/posts\/td_learning\/": {
        
        "title": "Temporal Difference Learning",
        "tags": [],
        "content": "Observe samples \\(\\left(s_t, a_t, r_t, s_{t+1} \\right)\\). If value estimates are accurate, the following must hold:\n\\begin{equation} V(s_t) = r_t + \\gamma V(s_{t+1}) \\end{equation}\nIf not, there is a TD error:\n\\begin{equation} \\gamma = r_t + \\gamma V(s_{t+1}) - V(s_t) \\end{equation}\nTo learn better estimates - minimize $γ $ TD(0):\n\\begin{equation} V(s) \\leftarrow V(s) + \\alpha \\left( r_t + \\gamma V(s_{t+1}) - V(s_t) \\right) \\end{equation}\n", 
        "url": "https:\/\/braindump.jethro.dev\/posts\/td_learning\/"
    },

        "tags": [],

        "url": "https:\/\/braindump.jethro.dev\/posts\/testing_sync_at_dropbox_dropbox\/"
    },
    
    
    
    "https:\/\/braindump.jethro.dev\/posts\/the_art_of_storytelling_pixar_in_a_box_partner_content_khan_academy\/": {
        
        "title": "The art of storytelling | Pixar in a Box | Partner content | Khan Academy",
        "tags": [],
        "content": " tags Storytelling  ", 
        "url": "https:\/\/braindump.jethro.dev\/posts\/the_art_of_storytelling_pixar_in_a_box_partner_content_khan_academy\/"

    "https:\/\/braindump.jethro.dev\/posts\/the_art_of_unix_programming\/": {
        
        "title": "The Art Of Unix Programming",
        "tags": [],
        "content": " title The Art Of Unix Programming author Ken Thompson personal rating 5/5 tags Books  In The Art of Unix Programming, Ken Thompson was quoted to have provided the following design rules:\n Build modular programs Write readable programs Use composition Separate mechanisms from policy Write simple programs Write small programs Write transparent programs Write robust programs Make data complicated when required, not the program Build on potential users’ expected knowledge Avoid unnecessary output Write programs which fail in a way easy to diagnose Value developer time over machine time Write abstract programs that generate code instead of writing code by hand Prototype software before polishing it Write flexible and open programs Make the program and protocols extensible  Particularly fascinating was the point on separating mechanisms from policy. The argument is that mechanisms don’t evolve as quickly as policies, hence coupling the two would make it difficult to improve upon the software without breaking the mechanism. Eric gave the example of X, and the survival of X was attributed to its mechanisms (the raster operations) being separated from the GUI implementations, which had been phased out multiple times.\n", 
        "url": "https:\/\/braindump.jethro.dev\/posts\/the_art_of_unix_programming\/"
    },
    
    
    
    "https:\/\/braindump.jethro.dev\/posts\/the_average_expert_is_as_accurate_as_a_dart_throwing_chimpanzee\/": {
        
        "title": "The Average Expert is as Accurate as A Dart-throwing Chimpanzee",
        "tags": [],
        "content": "The predictions we see on the news, and on television from \u0026ldquo;experts\u0026rdquo; are often simply stories told in a compelling fashion. These predictions rarely survive the test of time.\nHowever, there is a subset of experts that seem to be able predict the future better than the rest (see The Good Judgment Project).\n", 
        "url": "https:\/\/braindump.jethro.dev\/posts\/the_average_expert_is_as_accurate_as_a_dart_throwing_chimpanzee\/"
    },
    
    
    
    "https:\/\/braindump.jethro.dev\/posts\/bias_complexity_tradeoff\/": {
        
        "title": "The Bias-Complexity Tradeoff",
        "tags": ["machine-learning",],
        "content": "Training data can mislead the learner, and result in overfitting. To overcome this problem, we can restrict the search space to some hypothesis space \\(\\mathcal{H}\\). This can be seen as introducing prior knowledge to the learning task. Is such prior knowledge necessary?\nThe No-Free-Lunch Theorem The No-Free-Lunch theorem states that for binary classification tasks, for every learner there exists a distribution on which it fails. We say that the learner fails if, upon receiving i.i.d. examples from that distribution, its output hypothesis is likely to have a large risk, whereas for the same distribution, there exists another learner that will output a hypothesis with a small risk. In other words, every learner has tasks on which it fails while others succeed.\nTherefore, when approaching a particular learning problem, defined by some distribution \\(D\\), we should have some prior knowledge on \\(D\\). One type of such prior knowledge is that \\(D\\) comes from some specific parametric family of distributions. Another type of such prior knowledge is that there exists \\(h\\) in some predefined hypothesis class \\(H\\), such that \\(L_D(h) = 0\\).\nError Decomposition we can decompose the error of an \\(ERM_H\\) predictor into two components as follows. Let \\(h_S\\) be an \\(ERM_H\\) hypothesis. Then we can write:\n\\begin{equation} L_D(h_S) = \\epsilon_{\\textrm{app}} + \\epsilon_{\\textrm{est}} \\end{equation}\nwhere \\(\\epsilon_{\\textrm{app}} = \\textrm{min}_{h\\in H} L_D(h)=\\), and \\(\\epsilon_{\\textrm{est}} = L_D(h_S) - \\epsilon_{\\textrm{app}}\\).\nThe approximation error is the minimum risk achievable by a predictor in the hypothesis class. This term measures how much risk we have because we restrict ourselves to a specific class (how much inductive bias we have). This approximation error does not depend on the sample size, and is solely determined by the hypothesis class chosen.\nUnder the realizability assumption, the approximate error is zero. In the agnostic case, the approximation error can be large (it always includes the error of the Bayes optimal predictor).\nThe estimation error is the difference between the approximation error and the error achieved by the ERM predictor. The estimation error results because the empirical risk (training error) is only an estimate of the true risk.\nSince our goal is to minimize the total error, we face a tradeoff, called the bias-complexity tradeoff. Choosing \\(H\\) to be a very rich class decreases the approximation error, but may increase the estimation error, as a rich \\(H\\) might lead to overfitting. Learning theory studies how rich we can make \\(H\\) while still maintaining reasonable estimation error.\n", 
        "url": "https:\/\/braindump.jethro.dev\/posts\/bias_complexity_tradeoff\/"
    },
    
    
    
    "https:\/\/braindump.jethro.dev\/posts\/c_lang\/": {
