    "https:\/\/braindump.jethro.dev\/posts\/the_cost_of_context_switching\/": {
        
        "title": "The Cost of Context Switching",
        "tags": [],
        "content": "Minimizing context switching is one of the best ways to increase productivity. I see this phenomenon occur in the systems I implement:\n Getting Things Done (GTD): Separating the cognitive process into individual tasks: e.g. capturing, processing, doing. In Emacs, context switching is minimized by doing everything within Emacs itself: writing code, writing notes, reading email. Using Org-mode as the glue has been extremely helpful. In building srs.ly, I consider the importance of ease of prompt creation. This reduces context switching: e.g. from reading the article to hopping over to Anki and creating flashcards.  ", 
        "url": "https:\/\/braindump.jethro.dev\/posts\/the_cost_of_context_switching\/"
    },
    
    
    
    "https:\/\/braindump.jethro.dev\/posts\/the_credit_cycle\/": {
        
        "title": "The Credit Cycle",
        "tags": [],
        "content": "The credit cycle refers to the changes in the availability of credit or capital. This is one of the most volative cycles than has a fundamental influence on the economy. The credit window can go from open to shut in an instant. Why is credit so important?\n Credit is required to refinance maturing debt: companies and other economic units usually don\u0026rsquo;t pay off their debts, but roll them over Capital is an essential ingredient in the productive process, and is required to finance growth  A closed credit market causes fear to spread, often out of proportion to reality. Difficult conditions can cause the vicious cycles of closing and fear, leading to financial crises.\nHow does the credit cycle work?  The economy prospers Providers of capital thrive, increasing their capital base The scarcity of bad news results in perceived lowered risks in investing and lending Risk averseness lowers Financial institutions expand their business, providing more capital Financial institutions compete for market share by lowering demanded returns (e.g. cutting interest rates), lowering credit standards, and providing more capital per transaction Capital is destroyed: investments are made in projects where the cost of capital outweights the returns (if any) Losses cause lenders to be discouraged Risk averseness rises, and with it interest rates and restrictions Less capital is available, and only to the most qualified of borrowers Companies are starved for capital, unable to roll over debts and default This causes economic contraction  ", 
        "url": "https:\/\/braindump.jethro.dev\/posts\/the_credit_cycle\/"
    },
    
    
    
    "https:\/\/braindump.jethro.dev\/posts\/the_distressed_debt_cycle\/": {
        
        "title": "The Distressed Debt Cycle",
        "tags": [],
        "content": "Investors buy debt securities because they expect to be paid interest periodically and have their principal repaid when the debt matures.\nHowever, for distressed debt, the consensus is that this debt may not be repaid. The motivation behind purchasing the debt is then to have a \u0026ldquo;creditor claim\u0026rdquo; against the debtor: when the company declares bankruptcy, they gain ownership of the company, depending on the debt held. A distressed debt investor tries to figure out what a bankrupt company is worth, how this value will be divided amongst creditors, and how long this process will take.\nAs with other cycles (The Economic Cycle, The Credit Cycle, The Profit Cycle) the ability to obtain top returns from distressed debt is cyclical, and dependent on these other cycles.\nOpportunities to make returns here are created by the unwise extension of credit. Here\u0026rsquo;s an example with bonds:\n Stringent standards are applied to bonds This facilitates the servicing of debt, making defaulting scarce Having a combination of high yields and low risk makes bonds attractive, attracting capital to the bond market Increased capital translates to increased demand, resulting in increased bond issuance Larger amounts of bond issued leads to lowering of standards  Influences from other cycles include:\n A slowing economy affects investor psychology, company profitability (The Profit Cycle), and fluctuation in the credit market (The Credit Cycle) The Credit Cycle affects the availability of capital to refinance debt  ", 
        "url": "https:\/\/braindump.jethro.dev\/posts\/the_distressed_debt_cycle\/"
    },
    
    
    
    "https:\/\/braindump.jethro.dev\/posts\/the_economic_cycle\/": {
        
        "title": "The Economic Cycle",
        "tags": [],
        "content": " Currently in long-term secular uptrend because of:  macro environment corporate growth the borrowing mentality popularization of investing investor psychology    The output of an economy is the product of hours worked and rate of work done. These are affected by fundamental factors like birth rate which change little from year to year.\nFactors affecting short-term trend:\n Willingness to work (market conditions etc.) Fear of spending Amount of wealth: consumers are unlikely to fund consumption by selling stocks or homes  Most economic forecasts are just extrapolations of current trends: they are usually correct but not valuable. Unconventional forecasts (e.g. predicting deviations from the long-term trend) are usually incorrect but valuable\nSee also How Governments Affect the Economic Cycle and How Central Banks Affect the Economic Cycle.\nSources  Book: Mastering The Market Cycle  ", 
        "url": "https:\/\/braindump.jethro.dev\/posts\/the_economic_cycle\/"
    },
    
    
    
    "https:\/\/braindump.jethro.dev\/posts\/the_fermi_estimate\/": {
        
        "title": "The Fermi Estimate",
        "tags": [],
        "content": "Physicist Enrico Fermi was a master at guesstimation. Consider questions like these: What\u0026rsquo;s the size of the market for razors in China? How many piano tuners are there in Chicago? These questions are called Fermi problems.\n It is better to be approximately right, than precisely wrong\n Fermi tackles this problem by breaking down the problem, and performing back-of-envelope calculations. We tackle the problem of estimating the number of piano tuners in the city of Chicago. What numbers do we need to know?\n The number of people in Chicago The number of people who play the piano in Chicago The number of music schools in Chicago How often a piano needs to be tuned How long it takes to tune a piano  Often, getting an estimate within a factor of 10 or 20 is enough to make a decision.\nResources  Fermi Estimates - LessWrong  ", 
        "url": "https:\/\/braindump.jethro.dev\/posts\/the_fermi_estimate\/"
    },
    
    
    
    "https:\/\/braindump.jethro.dev\/posts\/the_good_judgment_project\/": {
        
        "title": "The Good Judgment Project",
        "tags": [],
        "content": "The Good Judgment Project (GJP) is a project \u0026ldquo;harnessing the wisdom of the crowd to forecast world events\u0026rdquo;.\nSimilar in virtue to the GJP is Metaculus.\n", 
        "url": "https:\/\/braindump.jethro.dev\/posts\/the_good_judgment_project\/"
    },
    
    
    
    "https:\/\/braindump.jethro.dev\/posts\/the_hidden_life_of_trees_what_they_feel_how_they_communicate\/": {
        
        "title": "The Hidden Life of Trees: What They Feel, How They Communicate",
        "tags": [],
        "content": " author Peter Wohlleben Goodreads https://www.goodreads.com/book/show/28256439-the-hidden-life-of-trees  Themes  Wood Wide Web  Interesting Facts  Some forests plan reproduction in advance, such as birch trees. This regulates many things, for example, the population of animals that depend on their fruit. Parent trees enforce a strict upbringing on the new generation of young seedlings. One way they do this is through light deprivation: young seedlings are short and covered by the crowns of the parent trees. The young trees often only receive enough light to survive, enforcing a slow growth, and encouraging them to develop extensive branches and leaves that can absorb the little sunlight they receive. Once the mother tree perishes, the young trees can then grow up strong. \u0026ldquo;A tree is only as strong as the forest\u0026rdquo;: Trees are social beings, and trees try to ensure that every tree in the forest is equally successful (e.g. their photosynthesis rate is often synchronized). The notion of having to space out the vegetation to avoid overcrowding comes from a forester\u0026rsquo;s mentality. In practice, trees don\u0026rsquo;t practice this sort of distancing.  ", 
        "url": "https:\/\/braindump.jethro.dev\/posts\/the_hidden_life_of_trees_what_they_feel_how_they_communicate\/"
    },
    
    
    
    "https:\/\/braindump.jethro.dev\/posts\/the_intelligent_investor\/": {
        
        "title": "The Intelligent Investor",
        "tags": [],
        "content": " author Benjamin Graham tags Finance  Book Notes Being an intelligent investor is about developing emotional discipline An intelligent investor refuses to stoop to the market\u0026rsquo;s level of irrationality. They minimize the odds of irreversible gains, and maximize the odds of achieving sustainable gains.\nInvestor vs Speculator The investor must recognize the existence of a speculative factor in their common-stock holdings, and keep this components within minor limits.\nThe defensive investor must confine himself to the shares of important companies with a long record of profitable operations and in strong financial condition.\n Always thoroughly analyse a company, and the soundness of the underlying businesses, before buying its stock. Deliberate protect yourself against serious losses aspire to \u0026ldquo;adequate\u0026rdquo;, not extraordinary performance  The stock market is riskier when prices are higher Stock market performance factors The performance of a stock market is dependent on 3 factors:\n Real growth (rise of company\u0026rsquo;s earnings and dividends) Inflationary growth (rise of prices throughout the economy) Speculative growth (increase or decrease in the investing public\u0026rsquo;s appetite for stocks)  Combating Inflation Mild inflation allows companies to pass the increased costs of their own raw materials on to customers, but high inflation wreaks havoc-forcing customers to slash their purchases, depressing activity throughout the economy.\nTo guard against inflation, a common advice is to buy stocks, but in truth stocks failed to keep up with inflation about one-fifth of the time.\nTo combat inflation, additionally look into REITs, and government bonds.\n", 
        "url": "https:\/\/braindump.jethro.dev\/posts\/the_intelligent_investor\/"
    },
    
    
    
    "https:\/\/braindump.jethro.dev\/posts\/the_law_of_diminishing_returns\/": {
        
        "title": "The Law of Diminishing Returns",
        "tags": [],
        "content": "All components of optionality face diminishing returns: after some point, the advantages gained start to level off. For example, as a newbie fitness training may reap massive gains, but the improvement slows as time passes.\nIn practice, this means that we need to be aware when we\u0026rsquo;re hitting diminishing returns, and redirect efforts towards areas of low-hanging fruit.\n", 
        "url": "https:\/\/braindump.jethro.dev\/posts\/the_law_of_diminishing_returns\/"
    },
    
    
    
    "https:\/\/braindump.jethro.dev\/posts\/the_learning_problem\/": {
        
        "title": "The Learning Problem",
        "tags": [],
        "content": "In Machine Learning, an agent is said to be learning if it improves its performance P on task T based on experience/observations/data E. T must be fixed, P must be measurable, E must exist. See Learning agents.\nTypes of Learning Active vs Passive Learning An active learner interacts with the environment at training time, (e.g. by posing queries or performing experiments), while a passive learner only observes the information provided by the environment.\nOnline vs Batch Learning In online learning, the hypothesis has to be updated each time a new label is received.\nReinforcement Learning Each action in a state has an associated cost and a probability distribution of the next state.\nGoal is to learn a policy (mapping from state to action) that minimizes the sum of expected current and future costs.\nSupervised Learning Since learning involves an interaction between the learner and the environment, one can divide tasks according to the nature of that interaction.\nSupervised learning describes a scenario in which the training examples contain significant information that is missing in the unseen \u0026ldquo;test examples\u0026rdquo;. In unsupervised learning, there is no distinction between the training and test data. The goal is to come up with some summary, or compressed version of that data.\nMeasure of success A loss function helps measure our success. Given a set \\(H\\) of hypothesis of models, and a domain \\(Z\\), let \\(l\\) be a function from \\(H \\times Z\\) to non-negative real numbers \\(l: H \\times Z \\rightarrow \\mathbb{R}_{+}\\).\nThe risk function is the expected loss of the hypothesis,\n\\begin{equation*} L_D(h) = E_{z \\sim D}[l(h,z)] \\end{equation*}\nWe are interested in finding a hypothesis \\(h\\) that has a small risk, or expected loss, typically using Empirical Risk Minimization.\nAssumptions Made ⚠  One common assumption is that the data in the data generation process is independently and identically distributed (IID), according to the distribution \\(D\\).  Q: Given a large enough training set, do you expect the long term test error to be similar to the training error?\n If IID, then yes If not, there is likely dependencies, but under certain conditions, yes.  If sampling mixes well, it will not take long for D\u0026rsquo; to look like a steady set distribution.   If dependencies are exploited, there is a possibility of attaining lower training and test error.  Is Learning Feasible? The target function \\(f\\) that we want to learn is unknown. The performance of a hypothesis on the training set \\(D\\) tells us nothing about the performance on the data outside of \\(D\\).\nAs long as \\(f\\) is unknown, knowing \\(D\\) cannot exclude any patterns of \\(f\\) outside of \\(D\\), and the predictions of \\(g\\) would be meaningless.\nProbabilistic View If we accept a probabilistic answer, that is \\(D\\) tells us something likely about \\(f\\) outside of \\(D\\), then learning is feasible, only with a small price.\nLearning a hypothesis \\(g\\) approximates the target function \\(f\\) well, i.e. \\(E_{out}(g) \\approx 0\\). However, probabilistic analysis via Hoeffding\u0026rsquo;s Inequality gives \\(E_{out}(g) \\approx E_{in}(g)\\). Therefore, we still need to ensure \\(E_{in}(g) \\approx 0\\).\n", 
        "url": "https:\/\/braindump.jethro.dev\/posts\/the_learning_problem\/"
    },
    
    
    
    "https:\/\/braindump.jethro.dev\/posts\/the_market_cycle\/": {
        
        "title": "The Market Cycle",

        "content": "Price of assets in the market are primarily affected by development in two areas:\n Fundamentals: earnings, cash flow, and their outlook Psychology: how investors feel about the fundamentals, their appetite for risk  ", 
        "url": "https:\/\/braindump.jethro.dev\/posts\/the_market_cycle\/"
    },

        "title": "The opt-out illusion - Technology - TLS",
        "tags": [],
        "content": " source https://www.the-tls.co.uk/articles/the-opt-out-illusion/ tags Privacy   Opting out is an illusion. The extraction of our behavioural data is non-negotiable.\n ",
