    "https:\/\/braindump.jethro.dev\/posts\/topic_modelling\/": {
        
        "title": "Topic Modeling",
        "tags": [],
        "content": " tags Machine Learning  http://www.cs.columbia.edu/~blei/topicmodeling.html LDA survey - Github\nLDA The Little Book on LDA https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=FkckgwMHP2s http://www.cs.columbia.edu/~blei/papers/Blei2012.pdf\nDirichlet Distribution https://www2.ee.washington.edu/techsite/papers/documents/UWEETR-2010-0006.pdf\nDirichlet distribution is a family of continuous multivariate probability distributions parameterized by a vector α of positive reals.\n\\begin{equation} \\theta \\sim Dir(\\alpha) \\end{equation}\n\\begin{equation} p(\\theta) = \\frac{1}{\\beta(\\alpha)} \\prod_{i=1}^n \\theta_i^{\\alpha_i-1} I(\\theta \\in S) \\end{equation}\nWhere \\(\\theta = (\\theta_1, \\theta_2, \\dots, \\theta_n), \\alpha = (\\alpha_1, \\alpha_2, \\dots, \\alpha_n), \\alpha_i \u0026gt; 0\\) and\n\\begin{equation} S = \\left\\{x \\in \\mathbb{R}^n : x_i \\ge 0, \\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i = 1 \\right\\} \\end{equation}\nand \\(\\frac{1}{\\beta(\\alpha)} = \\frac{\\Gamma(\\alpha_0)}{\\Gamma(\\alpha_1)\\Gamma(\\alpha_2)\\dots\\Gamma(\\alpha_n)}\\)\nThe infinite-dimensional generalization of the Dirichlet distribution is the Dirichlet process.\nThe Dirichlet distribution is the conjugate prior distribution of the categorical distribution (a generic discrete probability distribution with a given number of possible outcomes) and multinomial distribution (the distribution over observed counts of each possible category in a set of categorically distributed observations). This means that if a data point has either a categorical or multinomial distribution, and the prior distribution of the distribution\u0026rsquo;s parameter (the vector of probabilities that generates the data point) is distributed as a Dirichlet, then the posterior distribution of the parameter is also a Dirichlet.\nExploring a Corpus with the posterior distribution Quantities needed for exploring a corpus are the posterior expectations of hidden variables. Each of these quantities are conditioned on the observed corpus.\nVisualizing a topic is done by visualizing the posterior topics through their per-topic probabilities \\(\\hat{\\beta}\\).\nVisualizing a document uses the posterior topic proportions \\(\\hat{\\theta}_{d,k}\\) and the posterior topic assignments \\(\\hat{z}_{d,k}\\).\nFinding similar documents can be done through the Hellinger distance:\n\\begin{align*} D_{d,k} = \\sum_{k=1}^K \\left( \\sqrt{\\hat{\\theta}_{d,k}} - \\sqrt{\\hat{\\theta}_{f,k}}\\right)^2 \\end{align*}\nPosterior Inference    Mean Field Variational Inference\nApproximate intractable posterior distribution with a simpler distribution containing free variational parameters. These parameters are fit to approximate the true posterior.\nIn contrast to the true posterior, the mean field variational distribution for LDA is one where the variables are independent of each other, with and each governed by a different variational parameter.\nWe fit the variational parameters to minimise the KL-divergence to the true posterior.\nThe general approach to mean-field variational methods - update each variational parameter with the parameter given by the expectation of the true posterior under the variational distribution - is applicable when the conditional distribution of each variable is the exponential family.\n  Markov Chains http://setosa.io/ev/markov-chains/\nShortcomings  strong, potentially invalid statistical assumptions:  topics have no correlation to one another (dirichlet assumes nearly independent)  solution: CTM: use a logistic normal distribution   assumes order of documents don\u0026rsquo;t matter  solution: DTM: use logistic normal distribution to model topics evolving over time      TopicRNN http://www.columbia.edu/~jwp2128/Papers/DiengWangetal2017.pdf\nIn TopicRNN, latent topic models are used to capture global semantic dependencies so that the RNN can focus its modeling capacity on the local dynamics of the sequences\nPotential Research Topics TODO Visualization of Perplexity for topic models as a potential topic? ", 
        "url": "https:\/\/braindump.jethro.dev\/posts\/topic_modelling\/"
    },
    
    
    
    "https:\/\/braindump.jethro.dev\/posts\/transfer_learning\/": {
        
        "title": "Transfer Learning",
        "tags": [],
        "content": " tags Reinforcement Learning ⭐  Prior understanding of problem structure can help us solve complex tasks quickly. Perhaps solving prior tasks would help acquire useful knowledge for solving a new task.\nTransfer learning is the use of experience from one set of tasks for faster learning and better performance on a new task.\nFew-Shot Learning \u0026ldquo;shot\u0026rdquo; refers to the number of attempts in the target domain. For example, in 0-shot learning, a policy trained in the source domain works in the target domain.\nThis typically requires assumptions of similarity between source and target domain.\nTaxonomy of Transfer Learning  \u0026ldquo;forward\u0026rdquo; transfer train on one task, transfer to a new task  Randomizing source domain Fine-tuning   multi-task transfer train on many tasks, transfer to a new task  generate highly randomized source domains model-based RL model distillation contextual policies modular policy networks   multi-task meta-learning learn to learn from many tasks  RNN-based/Gradient-based meta-learning    Forward Transfer Fine-tuning Key Idea: Train on the source task, then train some more on the target task, for example, by retraining the weights on the last layer. Lower layers are likely to learn representations from the source task that are useful in the target task. This works well if the source task is broad and diverse.\nFine tuning is popular in the supervised learning setting.\nWhy fine-tuning doesn\u0026rsquo;t work well for RL  RL tasks tend to be narrow (not broad and diverse), and features are less general RL methods tend to learn deterministic policies, the policies that are optimal in the fully-observed MDP.  Low-entropy policies adapt very slowly to new settings Little exploration at convergence    To increase diversity and entropy, we can do maximum-entropy learning which acts as randomly as possible while collecting high rewards Haarnoja et al., n.d.:\n\\begin{equation} \\pi(a|s) = \\mathrm{exp} (Q_\\phi(s,a)-V(s)) \\end{equation}\nThis optimizes\n\\begin{equation} \\sum_t E_{\\pi(s_t, a_t)}[r(s_t, a_t)] + E_{\\pi(s_t)}[\\mathcal{H}(\\pi(a_t|s_t))] \\end{equation}\nManipulating the Source Domain This is used where we can design the source domain (e.g. training in a simulator, which can be tweaked). Injecting randomness/diversity in the source tends to be helpful.\nResources:\n Domain Randomization for Sim2Real Transfer  Multi-task Transfer Some things remain constant across tasks, such as the laws of physics. Model-based RL may learn these laws, and transfer this knowledge across multiple tasks.\nIn model distillation, multiple policies are combined into one, for concurrent multi-task learning. Policy distillation Rusu et al., n.d. is able to:\n Compress policies learnt on single games into smaller models Build agents capable of playing multiple games Improve the stability of the DQN learning algorithm by distilling online the policy of the best performing agent  \u0026lt;biblio.bib\u0026gt;\n", 
        "url": "https:\/\/braindump.jethro.dev\/posts\/transfer_learning\/"
    },
    
    
    
    "https:\/\/braindump.jethro.dev\/posts\/transformer_models\/": {
        
        "title": "Transformer Models",
        "tags": [],
        "content": "Transformer are model architectures that have proven effective across range of domains such as Natural Language Processing and Computer Vision.\nHow Transformers work To reduce sequential computation, many models use CNNs as building blocks., computing hidden representations in parallel for all input and output positions. However, the number of operations required to relate signals from two arbitrary input or output positions grows in the distance between the positions, making it difficult to learn dependencies between distant positions.\n*The transformer reduces this to a constant number of operations, at the cost of reduced effective resolution due to averaging attention-weighted positions. This effect is counteracted with multi-head attention.*\nThe Transformer model relies entirely on self-attention (or intra-attention) to compute representations of its input and output without using sequence-aligned RNNs or convolution.\nMaking Transformer Models Efficient The traditional Transformer model has memory and computational complexities that are quadratic with the input sequence length (\\(O(N^2)\\)). This limits the utility of Transformer models, since their main benefit is the ability to learn alignments across long sequences.\nEfficient transformer models attempt to alleviate the cost of computing the attention matrix, either by approximating the matrix, or by introducing sparsity. NO_ITEM_DATA:tayEfficientTransformersSurvey2020 provides a good overview of these efficient Transformer models. The key summary table in the paper is reproduced below.\n  Figure 1: Summary of Efficient Transformer Models\n  Transformer Architectures BART BART is a denoising auto-encoder for pretraining sequence-to-sequence models. BART is trained by:\n corrupting text with an arbitrary noising function learning a model to reconstruct the original text  It generalizes BERT and GPT. One key advantage of BART is that the noising function can be arbitrarily selected. For example, they introduce an in-filling scheme replacing arbitrary lengths of text with a single mask token. This generalizes the original word masking and next sentence prediction objectives.\nBART is trained by corrupting documents and then optimizing a reconstruction loss - the cross-entropy between the decoder\u0026rsquo;s output and the original document.\nBART can then be fine-tuned for downstream applications:\n sequence classification the same input is fed into the encoder and decoder, and the final decoder token and hidden state is fed into a new multi-class linear classifier. token classification feed the complete document into the encoder and decoder, and use the top hidden state of the decoder as a representation for each word. sequence generation input sequence is given to the encoder, and the decoder generates auto-regressively.  \u0026lt;biblio.bib\u0026gt;\nTODO T5 ", 
        "url": "https:\/\/braindump.jethro.dev\/posts\/transformer_models\/"
    },

    "https:\/\/braindump.jethro.dev\/posts\/trigger_list\/": {
        
        "title": "Trigger List",
        "tags": [],
        "content": "Trigger List Use this trigger list to recall items that are need require action:\n Projects started, not completed Projects that need to be started Commitments/promises to others  Teachers/TAs Roommates Classmates Friends \u0026ldquo;Outside\u0026rdquo; people  Parents Campus organizations Extracurricular organizations Graduates     Communications to make/get  Internal/External  Initiate or respond to:  Phone calls Text messages Voice-mail E-mail Snail mail Social Media     Other writing to finish/submit  Reports Papers Reviews Assignments Articles Readings Rewrites and edits     Meetings that need to be set/requested Waiting for\u0026hellip;  Information Delegated tasks/projects Completions critical to projects   Replies to:  Texts Calls Decisions of others    ", 
        "url": "https:\/\/braindump.jethro.dev\/posts\/trigger_list\/"
    },
    
    
    
    "https:\/\/braindump.jethro.dev\/posts\/two_levels_of_inference\/": {
        
        "title": "Two Levels Of Inference",
        "tags": [],
        "content": " tags Occam\u0026rsquo;s Razor  There are 2 levels of inference: model fitting and model comparison. In model fitting, assuming a model is true (say \\(\\mathcal{H}_i\\)), fit the model to the data by inferring what values its free parameters should possibly take.\n\\begin{equation} P\\left(\\mathbf{w} | D, \\mathcal{H}_{i}\\right)=\\frac{P\\left(D | \\mathbf{w}, \\mathcal{H}_{i}\\right) P\\left(\\mathbf{w} | \\mathcal{H}_{i}\\right)}{P\\left(D | \\mathcal{H}_{i}\\right)} \\end{equation}\nThe normalizing constant is irrelevant to the first level of inference. It is common to use gradient-based methods to find the maximum of the posterior \\(\\mathbf{w}_{\\text{MP}}\\). Error bars for these parameters can be obtained by evaluating the Hessian at \\(\\mathbf{w}_{\\text{MP}}\\), \\(\\mathbf{A}=-\\nabla \\nabla \\ln P\\left(\\mathbf{w} | D, \\mathcal{H}_{i}\\right)\\), and Taylor-expanding the log posterior probability with \\(\\Delta \\mathbf{w}=\\mathbf{w}-\\mathbf{w}_{\\mathrm{MP}}\\):\n\\begin{equation} P\\left(\\mathbf{w} | D, \\mathcal{H}_{i}\\right) \\simeq P\\left(\\mathbf{w}_{\\mathrm{MP}} | D, \\mathcal{H}_{i}\\right) \\exp \\left(-1 / 2 \\Delta \\mathbf{w}^{\\mathrm{T}} \\mathbf{A} \\Delta \\mathbf{w}\\right) \\end{equation}\nlocally approximating the posterior as a Gaussian with covariance matrix \\(\\mathbf{A}^{-1}\\).\nIn model comparison, we compare models in light of the data, assign some sort of preference.\nBayesian methods can consistently and quantitatively solve both types of inferences, although adopting the Bayesian method for the first type leads to similar results from orthodox statistical methods. Orthodox statistical methods will find it difficult to perform model comparisons, because it is not possible simply to choose the model that fits the data itself. For example, maximum likelihood can fail by choosing implausible, over-parameterized models that overfit the data.\nHow do Bayesian methods perform model comparison? The posterior probability for each model is:\n\\begin{equation} P\\left(\\mathcal{H}_{i} | D\\right) \\propto P\\left(D | \\mathcal{H}_{i}\\right) P\\left(\\mathcal{H}_{i}\\right) \\end{equation}\nHence, if we assign equal priors to the alternative models, models \\(\\mathcal{H}_i\\) are ranked by evaluating the evidence. If the posterior is well approximated by a Gaussian, Bayesian model comparison is a simple extension of maximum likelihood model selection: the evidence is obtained by multiplying the best-fit likelihood by the Occam factor, obtained from the determinant of the covariance matrix \\(\\mathbf{A}^{-1}\\) (the inverse Hessian).\n", 
        "url": "https:\/\/braindump.jethro.dev\/posts\/two_levels_of_inference\/"
    },
    
    
    
    "https:\/\/braindump.jethro.dev\/posts\/uncertainty_in_robotics\/": {
        
        "title": "Uncertainty in Robotics",
        "tags": [],
        "content": "Robotic applications increasingly deal with more unstructured environments. Robots that can perceive and deal with uncertainty are much more robust in these scenarios.\nUncertainty arises from:\n Environment unpredictability of the physical world Sensors limitations in sensor perception Robots robot actuations involve motors that have control noise Models models of the world inherently inaccurate Computation many algorithms are approximate   A robot that carries a notion of its own uncertainty that acts accordingly is superior to one that does not.\n Thrun, Burgard, and Fox, n.d.\nPros  Robust, and scale better to complex, unstructured environments Weaker requirements on the accuracy of the models compared to classical planning algorithms Sound methodology for many flavours of robot learning Broadly applicable to many problems, including perception and action  Cons  Relatively computationally inefficient Requires approximation (exact posteriors are computationally intractable)  ", 
        "url": "https:\/\/braindump.jethro.dev\/posts\/uncertainty_in_robotics\/"
    },
    
    
    
    "https:\/\/braindump.jethro.dev\/posts\/understanding_cycles\/": {
        
        "title": "Understanding Cycles",
        "tags": [],
        "content": " Cycles are not just a series of events in progression: they are often a progression of causative events Cyclical events are influenced by both endogenous events(cyclical events that precede them) and exogenous developments (occurring in other areas). A cycle\u0026rsquo;s clout is heightened by the inability of investors to remember the past Cycles are viewed as less symmetrical than they are: the negatives are usually exaggerated  Sources  Book: Mastering The Market Cycle  ", 
        "url": "https:\/\/braindump.jethro.dev\/posts\/understanding_cycles\/"
    },

    "https:\/\/braindump.jethro.dev\/posts\/vae\/": {
        
        "title": "Variational Autoencoders",
        "tags": [],
        "content": "Links  matthewvowels1/Awesome-VAEs  Variational Auto-encoder noise as encouraging code vectors to be far apart The VAE noise causes data points in the Z-space to be fuzzy, and may cause overlap. To minimize reconstruction error, the code vectors want to move away from each other. This doesn\u0026rsquo;t help!\nSo additional constraints are added to the system:\n Minimize square distances of balls to origin Make centre of mass zero Make size of balls close to 1 in each dimension  ", 
        "url": "https:\/\/braindump.jethro.dev\/posts\/vae\/"
    },
    
    
    
    "https:\/\/braindump.jethro.dev\/posts\/variational_inference\/": {
        
        "title": "Variational Inference",
        "tags": [],
        "content": " tags Machine Learning Algorithms  TODO Variational Bounds of Mutual Information (Poole et al., n.d.) TODO Black Box Variational Inference (Ranganath, Gerrish, and Blei, n.d.) TODO Unbiased Implicit Variational Inference (Titsias and Ruiz, n.d.) TODO A Contrastive Divergence for Combining Variational Inference and MCMC (Ruiz and Titsias, n.d.)  Bibliography Poole, Ben, Sherjil Ozair, Aaron van den Oord, Alexander A. Alemi, and George Tucker. n.d. “On Variational Bounds of Mutual Information.” http://arxiv.org/abs/1905.06922v1.\nRanganath, Rajesh, Sean Gerrish, and David M. Blei. n.d. “Black Box Variational Inference.” http://arxiv.org/abs/1401.0118v1.\nRuiz, Francisco J. R., and Michalis K. Titsias. n.d. “A Contrastive Divergence for Combining Variational Inference and Mcmc.” http://arxiv.org/abs/1905.04062v1.\nTitsias, Michalis K., and Francisco J. R. Ruiz. n.d. “Unbiased Implicit Variational Inference.” http://arxiv.org/abs/1808.02078v3.\n", 
        "url": "https:\/\/braindump.jethro.dev\/posts\/variational_inference\/"

    "https:\/\/braindump.jethro.dev\/posts\/vc_dimension\/": {
        
        "title": "VC-Dimension",
        "tags": [],
        "content": " tags Bias-Complexity Tradeoff, PAC Learning  Shalev-Shwartz and Ben-David, n.d.\nWhat makes one class learnable and another unlearnable? The family of learnable classes in the setup of binary valued classification with the zero-one loss relies on a combinatorial notion called the Vapnik-Chervonenkis dimension (VC-dimension).\nInfinite-size classes can be learnable To see that this is true, we provide a counterexample.\nlet \\(\\mathcal{H}\\) be the set of threshold functions over the real line, namely, \\(\\mathcal{H} = \\left\\{h_a : a \\in \\mathbb{R}\\right\\}\\), where \\(h_a : \\mathbb{R} \\rightarrow \\left\\{ 0,1\\rightarrow \\right\\}\\) is a function such that \\(h_a(x) = \\mathbb{I}_{[x \u0026lt; a]}\\). Clearly \\(H\\) is of infinite size. However, we can easily show that \\(\\mathcal{H}\\) is PAC learnable, with sample complexity:\n\\begin{equation} m_H(\\epsilon, \\delta) \\le \\lceil \\log (2/\\delta) / \\epsilon \\rceil \\end{equation}\nThe VC-dimension Hence, while finiteness of \\(\\mathcal{H}\\) is a sufficient condition for PAC learnability, it is not a necessary condition. Here we show that the VC-dimension of a hypothesis class gives the correct characterization of its learnability.\n Let \\(\\mathcal{H}\\) be a class of functions from \\(\\mathcal{X}\\) to \\(\\left\\{0,1\\right\\}\\), and let \\(C = \\{c_1, \\dots, c_m\\} \\subset X\\). The restriction of \\(\\mathcal{H}\\) to \\(C\\) is the set of functions from \\(C\\) to \\(\\{0, 1\\}\\) that can be derived from \\(\\mathcal{H}\\). That is:\n\\begin{equation} \\mathcal{H}_C = \\left\\{ h(c_1), \\dots, h(c_m) : h \\in \\mathcal{H} \\right\\} \\end{equation}\n where we represent each function from \\(C\\) to \\(\\{0, 1\\}\\) as a vector in \\(\\{0,1\\}^{|C|}\\).\n A hypothesis class \\(\\mathcal{H}\\) shatters a finite set \\(C \\subset \\mathcal{X}\\) if the restriction of \\(\\mathcal{H}\\) to \\(C\\) is the set of all functions from \\(C\\) to \\(\\{0, 1\\}\\). That is, \\(|\\mathcal{H}_C| = 2^{|C|}\\).\n Whenever some set \\(C\\) is shattered by \\(\\mathcal{H}\\), the adversary is not restricted by \\(\\mathcal{H}\\), as they can construct a distribution over \\(C\\) based on any target function from \\(C\\) to \\(\\{0,1\\}\\), while still maintaining the realizability assumption.\nThis leads us to the definition of VC-dimension:\n The VC-dimension of a hypothesis class \\(\\mathcal{H}\\), denoted \\(\\textrm{VCdim}(\\mathcal{H})\\), is the maximal size of a set \\(C \\subset \\mathcal{X}\\) that can be shattered by \\(\\mathcal{H}\\). If \\(\\mathcal{H}\\) can shatter \\(C\\) of any arbitrary size, then \\(\\mathcal{H}\\) has infinite VC-dimension.\n Examples Threshold Functions Let \\(\\mathcal{H}\\) be the class of threshold functions over \\(\\mathbb{R}\\). We have shown that for an arbitrary set \\(C = \\{c_1\\}\\), \\(\\mathcal{H}\\) shatters \\(C\\). However, we have shown that for an arbitrary set \\(C = \\{c_1, c_2\\}\\) where \\(c_1 \\le c_2\\), \\(\\mathcal{H}\\) does not shatter \\(C\\). Hence \\(\\textrm{VCdim}(\\mathcal{H}) = 1\\).\nIntervals Take \\(C = {1, 2}\\), and we see that \\(\\mathcal{H}\\) shatters \\(C\\). Hence \\(\\textrm{VCdim}(\\mathcal{H}) \\ge 2\\). However, take an arbitrary set \\(C = \\{c_1, c_2, c_3\\}\\) where \\(c_1 \\le c_2 \\le c_3\\). Then the labelling (1,0,1) cannot be obtained by an interval. Therefore, \\(\\textrm{VCdim}(\\mathcal{H}) = 2\\).\nThe Fundamental Theorem of Statistical Learning Let \\(\\mathcal{H}\\) be a hypothesis class of functions from a domain \\(\\mathcal{X}\\) to \\(\\{0, 1\\}\\) and let the loss function be the 0-1 loss. Then the following are equivalent:\n \\(\\mathcal{H}\\) has the uniform convergence property. Any ERM rule is a successful agnostic PAC learner for \\(\\mathcal{H}\\). \\(\\mathcal{H}\\) is agnostic PAC learnable. \\(\\mathcal{H}\\) is PAC learnable. Any ERM rule is a successful PAC learner for \\(\\mathcal{H}\\). \\(\\mathcal{H}\\) has a finite VC-dimension.  Sauer\u0026rsquo;s Lemma and the Growth Function We have defined the notion of shattering, by considering the restriction of \\(\\mathcal{H}\\) to a finite set of instances. The growth function measures the maximal \u0026ldquo;effective\u0026rdquo; size of \\(\\mathcal{H}\\) on a set of \\(m\\) examples. Formally:\n Let \\(\\mathcal{H}\\) be a hypothesis class. Then the growth function of \\(\\mathcal{H}\\), denoted \\(\\tau_{\\mathcal{H}}(m) : \\mathbb{N} \\rightarrow \\mathbb{N}\\), is defined as:\n\\begin{equation} \\tau_{\\mathcal{H}}(m) = \\textrm{max}_{C \\subset \\mathcal{X} : |C| = m} |\\mathcal{H}_C| \\end{equation}\n \\(\\tau_{\\mathcal{H}}(m)\\) is the number of different functions from a set \\(C\\) of size \\(m\\) to \\(\\{0,1\\}\\) that can be obtained by restricting \\(\\mathcal{H}\\) to \\(C\\). With this definition we can now state Sauer\u0026rsquo;s lemma:\n Let \\(\\mathcal{H}\\) be a hypothesis class with \\(\\textrm{VCdim}(\\mathcal{H}) \\le d \u0026lt; \\infty\\). Then for all \\(m\\),\n\\begin{equation} \\tau_{\\mathcal{H}}(m) \\le \\sum_{i=0}^{d}{m \\choose i} \\end{equation}\nIn particular, if \\(m \u0026gt; d + 1\\), then \\(\\tau_{\\mathcal{H}}(m) \\le (em/d)^d\\).\n \u0026lt;biblio.bib\u0026gt;\n", 
        "url": "https:\/\/braindump.jethro.dev\/posts\/vc_dimension\/"
    },
    
    
    
    "https:\/\/braindump.jethro.dev\/posts\/velocity_motion_model\/": {
        
        "title": "Velocity Motion Model",
        "tags": [],
        "content": " tags Odometry Motion Model, Motion Model With Maps  The velocity motion model assumes that the robot is controlled through 2 velocities: rotational and translational velocity:\n\\begin{equation} u_{t}=\\left(\\begin{array}{l}{v_{t}} \\ {\\omega_{t}}\\end{array}\\right) \\end{equation}\nA sampling algorithm generates a random pose according to the distribution \\(p(x_t| u_t, x_{t-1})\\), and perturbs it with the noise, drawn from the error parameters of the kinematic motion model.\n\\begin{equation} \\left(\\begin{array}{c}{\\hat{v}} \\ {\\hat{\\omega}}\\end{array}\\right)=\\left(\\begin{array}{c}{v} \\ {\\omega}\\end{array}\\right)+\\left(\\begin{array}{c}{\\varepsilon_{\\alpha_{1}|v|+\\alpha_{2}|\\omega|}} \\ {\\varepsilon_{\\alpha_{3}|v|+\\alpha_{4}|\\omega|}}\\end{array}\\right) \\end{equation}\n", 
        "url": "https:\/\/braindump.jethro.dev\/posts\/velocity_motion_model\/"
    },

    "https:\/\/braindump.jethro.dev\/posts\/visual_basic\/": {
        
        "title": "Visual Basic",
        "tags": [],
        "content": "Variables    Concept Explanation Examples     Variables Use Dim to declare variables Dim Name As String = \u0026quot;lijin\u0026quot;     Dim Year as Integer = 2020   Arrays Arrays have brackets beside them Dim Arr() As String = {\u0026quot;OCBC\u0026quot;, \u0026quot;UOB\u0026quot;}    Conditionals If Val \u0026gt; 2020 Then Console.WriteLine(\u0026quot;Value more than 2020\u0026quot;) Else Console.WriteLine(\u0026quot;Value less than 2020\u0026quot;) End If Dim Apple as Integer = 5 Select Case Apple Case 1 Console.WriteLine(\u0026quot;I have 1 apple.\u0026quot;) Case 2 Console.WriteLine(\u0026quot;I have 2 apples.\u0026quot;) Case 5 Console.WriteLine(\u0026quot;I have 5 apples.\u0026quot;) Case Else Console.WriteLine(\u0026quot;Not sure how many apples.\u0026quot;) End Select Looping Dim A as Integer = 1 While A \u0026lt; 10 A += 1 End While Console.WriteLine(A) For i = 1 to 10 Console.WriteLine(i) Next For Each item As String In groceryItems Console.WriteLine(item) Next Methods, Subroutines and Classes Subroutines do not return values, while methods can. Classes can contain methods.\nPublic Sub Fibonacci(ByVal n As Integer) ... End Sub Public Sub Add (ByVal a As Integer, ByVal b As Integer) ... End Sub Public Function returnsOne() As Integer Return 1 End Function Public Class Calculator Public Function Add(ByVal a As Integer, ByVal b As Integer) Return a + b End Function End Class Dim calc As New Calculator() calc.Add(1, 2) For excel specific functions, see here.\n", 
        "url": "https:\/\/braindump.jethro.dev\/posts\/visual_basic\/"
    },

    "https:\/\/braindump.jethro.dev\/posts\/what_happens_when_we_type_a_simple_command_on_shell\/": {
        
        "title": "What happens when we type a simple command on shell?",
        "tags": [],
        "content": " tags Operating Systems  -source :: https://unix.stackexchange.com/questions/123457/what-happens-when-we-type-a-simple-command-on-shell\nThe shell is a program, that uses 2 important native C system calls: fork() and exec(). These 2 system calls are used in the creation of subprocesses.\nFork() Fork creates a copy of the calling process as its child. This is how most processes (except for init) begins.\nExec() Exec refers to a family of functions that replace the current image with a new process image. It replaces defining parts of the current process\u0026rsquo;s memory stack with new parts loaded from an executable (e.g. /bin/ls).\nfork() is usually a necessary first step, otherwise the calling process will be replaced and no new process will be created.\nIsn\u0026rsquo;t this inefficient? No. The copy produced by the fork() is an abstraction, as the kernel uses a copy-on-write system. fork() creates a virtual memory map. If the copy is immediately followed by exec(), parts that are overwritten need not be copied. In addition, many parts of the child process (e.g. its environment) need not be duplicated.\n", 
        "url": "https:\/\/braindump.jethro.dev\/posts\/what_happens_when_we_type_a_simple_command_on_shell\/"
    },
    
    
    
    "https:\/\/braindump.jethro.dev\/posts\/what_to_write_down_when_you_re_reading_to_learn_aceso_under_glass\/": {
        
        "title": "What to write down when you’re reading to learn – Aceso Under Glass",
        "tags": [],
        "content": " source https://acesounderglass.com/2020/06/10/what-to-write-down-when-youre-reading-to-learn/ tags Zettelkasten  For deep research, write down things you find interesting. A good indication of what\u0026rsquo;s interesting is when an author makes you pause to think. Write down things that you\u0026rsquo;re thinking about.\n", 
        "url": "https:\/\/braindump.jethro.dev\/posts\/what_to_write_down_when_you_re_reading_to_learn_aceso_under_glass\/"
    },
    
    
    
    "https:\/\/braindump.jethro.dev\/posts\/when_bloom_filters_dont_bloom\/": {
        
        "title": "When Bloom filters don\x27t bloom",
        "tags": [],
        "content": "Modern CPUs are really good at sequential memory access, whet it\u0026rsquo;s possible to predict memory fetch patterns. Random memory access is very costly.\nModern computers require cache-optimized algorithms. When working with large datasets, not fitting L3 cache, prefer optimizing for reduced number loads, over optimizing the amount of memory used.\nBloom filters are great, as long as they fit into the L3 cache. They are optimized for memory usage, not memory access. A nice alternative is Cuckoo Filters.\n", 
        "url": "https:\/\/braindump.jethro.dev\/posts\/when_bloom_filters_dont_bloom\/"
    },

        "tags": [],

        "url": "https:\/\/braindump.jethro.dev\/posts\/feltcoop_why_svelte_why_svelte_is_our_choice_for_a_large_web_project_in_2020\/"
    },
    
    
    
    "https:\/\/braindump.jethro.dev\/posts\/wisdom\/": {
        
        "title": "Wisdom",
        "tags": [],
        "content": "Joe Rogan Experience #1309 - Naval Ravikant  The greatest artists are able to start over. If you\u0026rsquo;re not doing that, you\u0026rsquo;re just getting older. When it\u0026rsquo;s a dead-end, it\u0026rsquo;s the right thing to do, even when you feel like you\u0026rsquo;re almost there. We tend to define people by narrow identities, even though people are broader than that. Books read is a vanity metric. Read whatever satisfies your general curiousity, and for understanding. Look for ideas, things you don\u0026rsquo;t understand, and research it. We\u0026rsquo;re always signalling, looking at what other people see of us, rather than looking at what we are. Social media is making celebrities of all of us, and celebrities are the among the most miserable people in the world. Social contracts are powerful. When you tell people what you\u0026rsquo;re going to do, you\u0026rsquo;re generally serious about it. The retirement that people want is not being old and sitting in a nursing home. Retirement is when you stop sacrificing today for something imaginary tomorrow. When you\u0026rsquo;re unhappy, look at the underlying desire that in unfulfilled. It\u0026rsquo;s okay to have desires, but not too many. Pick the one overwhelming desire. To achieve peak performance, you must need to learn to optimize both the body and the mind. Keeping calm involves letting go of desires. Intellectual athletes need to work and rest. Cranking out work everyday is not for humans, it\u0026rsquo;s for machines. You\u0026rsquo;re not going to get rich renting out your time. You need to own a piece of business to gain your financial freedom. In the hunter-gatherer age, we worked for ourselves. The industrial age created a model of thousands of people working on one thing. If somebody can tell you when you have to go to work, what you have to wear, you\u0026rsquo;re not truly free. Coase Theorem (??)  \u0026ldquo;How To Be Successful (At Your Career, Twitter Edition)\u0026quot; ", 
        "url": "https:\/\/braindump.jethro.dev\/posts\/wisdom\/"
    },
    
    
    
    "https:\/\/braindump.jethro.dev\/posts\/wood_wide_web\/": {
        
        "title": "Wood Wide Web",
        "tags": [],
        "content": "Trees are able to establish working relationships with fungi. In return for a portion of their food production, fungi that attach to the roots of trees can help disseminate crucial information and perform nutrient exchange between trees in a forest. In many senses, the fungi act as the Internet for trees.\nOne interesting observation was that this behaviour was lesser seen in plantations (seeded trees), since the trees are harvested soon after.\nThe large, extensive, forest-wide connection established has been coined as \u0026ldquo;the Wood Wide Web\u0026rdquo;.\n", 
        "url": "https:\/\/braindump.jethro.dev\/posts\/wood_wide_web\/"
    },
    
    
    
    "https:\/\/braindump.jethro.dev\/posts\/work_clean\/": {
        
        "title": "Work Clean",
        "tags": [],
        "content": " title Work Clean author Dan Charnas tags Productivity  Preparation  Always turn up early, greet the day Calculate Meeze point  Optimal number of Actions done per day    Process  Optimize the workplace:  Put pens etc near dominant hand, not so important things within arms reach. This is to prevent hands from crossing over, reducing friction. Think about all the pain points, and think about how to automate them. Make checklists for recurring tasks. Checklists are immutable, unlike task lists.   Difference between immersive time and process time  The first minutes matter more than the last process time sometimes has more value Assign a block of 30min in the morning for process time, then alternate between immersive and process time   Clean as you go  Remove distractions from workplace Close emails etc as you go Email mark and sweep process:  Look through once, flagging and marking all as read Revisit flagged ones, take action if any Unflag when done     Slow down to move faster  Deliberately slow down to access quality velocity Maintain some forward momentum   Aim to finish/keep the finish in mind  Do effort estimates, and try to keep tasks small, so that they\u0026rsquo;re completable in shorter bursts Clock in time, take deliberate breaks in between, weigh the value of a pause in between work    ", 
        "url": "https:\/\/braindump.jethro.dev\/posts\/work_clean\/"
    },

    "https:\/\/braindump.jethro.dev\/posts\/writing_books\/": {
        
        "title": "Writing Books",
        "tags": [],
        "content": " tags Writing  Scott Meyers: Advice to Prospective Book Authors\nI wrote a book - DEV Community 👩‍💻👨‍💻\nWriting a Technical Book – zwischenzugs\nhttps://performancejs.com/post/31b361c/13-Tips-for-Writing-a-Technical-Book\n",

    "https:\/\/braindump.jethro.dev\/posts\/writing_good_spaced_repetition_prompts\/": {
        
        "title": "Writing Good Spaced Repetition Prompts",
        "tags": [],
        "content": "A spaced repetition repetition system is only as good as its prompts. To understand what a good prompt entails, it helps to first understand the operating principles behind a prompt.\nGood prompts invoke retrieval practice When you attempt to retrieve a piece of knowledge, the act of retrieval reinforces these memories. Improved recall also translates into better understanding and problem solving abilities 1.\nEffective prompts invoke retrieval practice well. In practice, this comes down to obeying a few guiding principles:\n Prompts should be focused, and test one detail at a time. Prompts should be precise, and have explicit, correct answers. Prompts should be tractable, with difficult prompts made easier via cues. Prompts should be effortful, requiring the actual task of retrieval.  Andy\u0026rsquo;s article gives a rough guide to creating meaningful prompts on a recipe of chicken stock, based on different kinds of knowledge.\nMichael Nielsen\u0026rsquo;s article talks about how he uses Anki to do shallow reads of scientific literature. In summary, he creates Anki flashcards for the key ideas and contributions of a paper, looking primarily at the abstract, conclusion, and some of the key figures of the paper.\nReferences  How to write good prompts: using spaced repetition to create understanding Spaced Repetition for Efficient Learning - Gwern    If the flashcards are numerous and vary enough around a concept, the mind fallsback to a typical problem-solving approach, rather than trying to memorize the answers. \u0026#x21a9;\u0026#xfe0e;\n   ", 
        "url": "https:\/\/braindump.jethro.dev\/posts\/writing_good_spaced_repetition_prompts\/"
    },
    
    
    
    "https:\/\/braindump.jethro.dev\/posts\/writing_with_zettekasten\/": {
        
        "title": "Writing with Zettekasten",
        "tags": [],
        "content": "The status quo for writing is:\n Find topic/research question Research/find literature Read and take notes Draw conclusions / outline text Write  This process is extremely linear. The student actually has to do many other things that aren\u0026rsquo;t part of writing the paper, and that has to happen before the process of writing begins.\nIf we flip this process in Zettelkasten, and make the notes the thinking process rather than a record of of the thinking process.\n Only written down ideas count Bottom-up Questions arise from material Categories emerge Externalized system to think in Highly standardized Process oriented  As a result:\n Thinking, connecting and understanding become concrete actions Focus is on the process, not the outcome Writing is broken down into reasonable steps (one note at a time) The value of each idea compounds There is a clear distinction between permanent and temporary notes  ", 
        "url": "https:\/\/braindump.jethro.dev\/posts\/writing_with_zettekasten\/"
    },
    
    
    
    "https:\/\/braindump.jethro.dev\/posts\/wu_stochastic_2020\/": {
        
        "title": "wu_stochastic_2020: Stochastic Normalizing Flows",
        "tags": [],
        "content": "Stochastic Normalizing Flows SNFs can be trained without computing exact generation probabilities by invoking ideas from nonequilibrium statistical mechanics ", 
        "url": "https:\/\/braindump.jethro.dev\/posts\/wu_stochastic_2020\/"
    },
    
    
    
    "https:\/\/braindump.jethro.dev\/posts\/xgboost\/": {
        
        "title": "XGBoost",
        "tags": [],
        "content": "XGboost is an end-to-end boosting system. It is sparsity-aware. (Chen and Guestrin, n.d.)\nRegularized Learning Objective For a given data set of \\(n\\) examples and \\(m\\) features, a tree ensemble model uses \\(K\\) additive functions to predict the output:\n\\begin{equation} \\hat{y}_i = \\phi(\\mathbf{x_i}) = \\sum_{k=1}^{K} f_k(\\mathbf{x_i}), f_k \\in F \\end{equation}\nwhere \\(F\\) is the space of regression trees, Each regression tree contains a continuous score on each of the leaf.\n  source: https://books.google.com.sg/books/about/Classification%5Fand%5FRegression%5FTrees.html?id=JwQx-WOmSyQC\u0026amp;redir%5Fesc=y\nWe wish to minimise the regularized objective:\n\\begin{equation} L(\\phi) = \\sum_i l(\\hat{y}_i, y_i) + \\sum_{k} \\Omega(f_k) \\end{equation}\nwhere\n\\begin{equation} \\Omega(f) = \\gamma T + \\frac{1}{2} \\lambda \\lVert w \\rVert^2 \\end{equation}\n\\(T\\) being the number of leaves in the tree, and \\(w_i\\) being the ith weight of the leaf.\nGradient Tree Boosting Since the regularized objective contains functions, the objective cannot be optimized in the Euclidean space.\nLet \\(\\hat{y}_i^t\\) be the prediction of the ith instance on the $t$-th iteration. We need to add \\(f_t\\) to minimize the following objective:\n\\begin{equation} L^{(t)} = \\sum_{i=1}^{n} l(y_i, \\hat{y}_i^{(t-1)} + f_t(\\mathbf{x}_i)) + \\Omega(f_t) \\end{equation}\nWe can perform a Taylor expansion, and obtain:\n\\begin{equation} L^{(t)} = \\sum_{i=1}^{n} l(y_i, \\hat{y}_i^{(t-1)}) + g_i f_t(\\mathbf{x}_i) + \\frac{1}{2}h_i f_t^2(\\mathbf{x}_i) + \\Omega(f_t) \\end{equation}\nwhere \\(g_i = \\partial_{\\hat{y}^{(t-1)}} l(y_i, \\hat{y}^{(t-1)})\\), and \\(h_i = \\partial^2_{\\hat{y}^{(t-1)}} l(y_i, \\hat{y}^{(t-1)})\\) are the first and second order gradient statistics (gradients and Hessians). Removing the constant terms, at step \\(t\\), the objective is simplified to:\n\\begin{equation} \\tilde{L}^{(t)} = \\sum_{i=1}^{n} g_i f_t(\\mathbf{x}_i) + \\frac{1}{2}h_i f_t^2(\\mathbf{x}_i) + \\Omega(f_t) \\end{equation}\nIf we define \\(I_j = \\{ i | q (x_i) = j\\}\\) as the instance set of leaf \\(j\\), then we can rewrite:\n\\begin{equation} \\tilde{L}^{(t)} = \\sum_{j=1}^T [(\\sum_{i \\in I_j} g_i) w_j + \\frac{1}{2} (\\sum_{i \\in I_j} h_i + \\lambda) w_j^2] + \\gamma T \\end{equation}\nand we find that for a fixed structure \\(q(x)\\), we can compute the optimal weight \\(w_j^*\\) of leaf \\(j\\):\n\\begin{equation} w_j^{*} = - \\frac{\\sum_{i \\in I_j} g_i}{\\sum_{i \\in I_j} h_i + \\lambda} \\end{equation}\nThis can then be used to score a tree structure \\(q\\). However, since enumerating all possible trees is computationally expensive, we use an algorithm to iteratively add branches to the tree, by computing the loss reduction from splitting:\n\\begin{equation} L_{\\text{split}} = \\frac{1}{2} \\left[ \\frac{\\left( \\sum_{i \\in I_L} g_i \\right)^2}{\\sum_{i \\in I_L} h_i + \\lambda} + \\frac{\\left( \\sum_{i \\in I_R} g_i \\right)^2}{\\sum_{i \\in I_R} h_i + \\lambda} - \\frac{\\left( \\sum_{i \\in I} g_i \\right)^2}{\\sum_{i \\in I} h_i + \\lambda} \\right] \\end{equation}\nImplementing Distributed XGBoost The implementation of distributed XGBoost uses RABIT, and the Allreduce framework. XGBoost requires gradients and hessians from each distributed worker. This fit the allreduce framework, which broadcasts each worker\u0026rsquo;s reduce result across all processes. (Chen, Cano, and Zhou, n.d.)\nBibliography Chen, Tianqi, and Carlos Guestrin. n.d. “Xgboost: A Scalable Tree Boosting System.” http://arxiv.org/abs/1603.02754v3.\nChen, Tianqi, Ignacio Cano, and Tianyi Zhou. n.d. “RABIT: A Reliable Allreduce and Broadcast Interface” 3 (2).\n", 
        "url": "https:\/\/braindump.jethro.dev\/posts\/xgboost\/"
    },
    
    
    
    "https:\/\/braindump.jethro.dev\/posts\/you_and_your_research\/": {
        
        "title": "You and Your Research - Richard Hamming",
        "tags": [],
        "content": " source http://www.cs.virginia.edu/~robins/YouAndYourResearch.html author Richard Hamming   Our society frowns on people who set out to do really good work. You\u0026rsquo;re not supposed to; luck is supposed to descend on you and you do great things by chance. \u0026ndash; Richard Hamming\n   Luck favours the prepared mind \u0026ndash; Pasteur\n One of the characteristics of successful scientists is having courage. They will go forward under incredible circumstances, they think and continue to think.\nMany mathematicians, theoretical physicists and astrophysicists do their best work when they are young. When you are famous, it is hard to work on small problems. Great scientists often make this error. After information theory, what could Shannon possibly do for an encore?\nIt is not sufficient to do a good job, you have to sell it. You have to learn to write clearly and well so people will read it, you must learn to give reasonably formal talks, and you also must learn to give informal talks.\nThe people who do great work with less ability but who are committed to it, get more done than those who have great skill and dabble in it.\nGood scientists will fight the system rather than learn to work with the systm and take advantage of all the system has to offer. Good scientists are also optimistic, on the lookout for the positive things, instead of the negative.\nIn conclusion:\n I claim that some of the reasons why so many people who have greatness within their grasp don\u0026rsquo;t succeed are: they don\u0026rsquo;t work on important problems, they don\u0026rsquo;t become emotionally involved, they don\u0026rsquo;t try and change what is difficult to some other situation which is easily done but is still important, and they keep giving themselves alibis why they don\u0026rsquo;t. \u0026ndash; Richard Hamming\n ", 
        "url": "https:\/\/braindump.jethro.dev\/posts\/you_and_your_research\/"
    },
    
    
    
    "https:\/\/braindump.jethro.dev\/posts\/zeigarnik_effect\/": {
        
        "title": "Zeigarnik Effect",
        "tags": [],
        "content": "Simply put, the Zeigarnik effect is the observation that interrupted tasks are better remembered than completed ones. This has ramifications for productivity.\nAn example of applying the Zeigarnik Effect would be to stop coding halfway while having a good streak, and come back another day.\n", 
        "url": "https:\/\/braindump.jethro.dev\/posts\/zeigarnik_effect\/"
    },
    
    
    
    "https:\/\/braindump.jethro.dev\/posts\/zeroshot_learning\/": {
        
        "title": "Zero shot Learning",
        "tags": [],
        "content": "Zero shot learning refers to recognizing a concept without having explicitly seen any examples of it.\n", 
        "url": "https:\/\/braindump.jethro.dev\/posts\/zeroshot_learning\/"
    },
    
    
    
    "https:\/\/braindump.jethro.dev\/posts\/zettelkasten\/": {
        
        "title": "Zettelkasten",
        "tags": [],
        "content": " tags Productivity, Note-taking  The original Zettelkasten Method In the morning, Luhmann would take notes from his readings, penning them down onto a piece of paper with a source inside.\nIn the evening, he would go through these notes and expand on them, writing about how they impact his current studies.\nIndexes in Zettelkasten Indexes have very few terms. They don\u0026rsquo;t serve as a broad overview of a topic, but more of an entry point.\nZettelkasten Note Types  fleeting notes taken as a reminder of what\u0026rsquo;s in your head. These are to be removed after some time  In Roam Research and Org-roam, make fleeting notes in daily notes   project-related notes related to a project permanent notes that are standalone, and make sense in their own context.  Arguments Against Zettelkasten  Densely interconnecting ideas across domains results in complex mental models, may obscure reality (Source: Venkatesh Rao, 2016 podcast with Farnam Street)  Why Zettelkasten  Forces you to adopt a linking system: connect ideas, tangents Break ideas up into smaller pieces, increase surface area for expansion  The Zettelkasten Method - LessWrong 2.0\n", 
        "url": "https:\/\/braindump.jethro.dev\/posts\/zettelkasten\/"
    },
    
    
    
    "https:\/\/braindump.jethro.dev\/posts\/zhu_ev_flownet_2018\/": {
        
        "title": "zhu_ev-flownet_2018: EV-FlowNet: self-supervised optical flow estimation for event-based cameras",
        "tags": [],
        "content": "EV-FlowNet: self-supervised optical flow estimation for event-based cameras Event data is well-suited for Optical Flow Estimation By directly measuring the precise time at which the pixel changes, the event stream encodes fine-grained motion information. For example, optical flow can be estimated from a local window around each event in a linear fashion.\nKey Ideas The authors propose a novel representation of event data, as an image with channels representing the number of events, and the latest timestamp at each polarity at each pixel. This preserves spatial relationships between events, while maintaining the most recent temporal information at each pixel, and providing a fixed number of channels for any event stream.\nThey also introduced a Self-supervised Learning method, using a photometric loss as a supervisory signal, without supervision from ground-truth flow.\nEvent Representation The event stream is represented as a 4 channel image:\n Number of positive polarity events Number of negative polarity events Timestamp of most recent positive event Timestamp of most negative event  This is similar to \u0026ldquo;Event-based Time Surfaces\u0026rdquo; (Lagorce et al., n.d.) and \u0026ldquo;Timestamp Images\u0026rdquo; (Park et al., n.d.)\nBibliography Lagorce, Xavier, Garrick Orchard, Francesco Galluppi, Bertram E. Shi, and Ryad B. Benosman. n.d. “HOTS: A Hierarchy of Event-Based Time-Surfaces for Pattern Recognition” 39 (7):1346–59. https://doi.org/10.1109/TPAMI.2016.2574707.\nPark, Paul K. J., Baek Hwan Cho, Jin Man Park, Kyoobin Lee, Ha Young Kim, Hyo Ah Kang, Hyun Goo Lee, et al. n.d. “Performance Improvement of Deep Learning Based Gesture Recognition Using Spatiotemporal Demosaicing Technique.” In 2016 IEEE International Conference on Image Processing (ICIP), 1624–28. https://doi.org/10.1109/ICIP.2016.7532633.\n", 
        "url": "https:\/\/braindump.jethro.dev\/posts\/zhu_ev_flownet_2018\/"
    },
    
    
    
    "https:\/\/braindump.jethro.dev\/posts\/zhu_unsupervised_2018\/": {
        
        "title": "zhu_unsupervised_2018: Unsupervised Event-based Learning of Optical Flow, Depth, and Egomotion",
        "tags": [],
        "content": "Unsupervised Event-based Learning of Optical Flow, Depth, and Egomotion Contributions The authors propose a new input representation that captures the spatiotemporal distribution of events, and a set of unsupervised loss functions that allows for learning of motion information only from the event stream.\nInput Representation Given a set of \\(N\\) input events \\(\\left\\{\\left(x_{i}, y_{i}, t_{i}, p_{i}\\right)\\right\\}_{i \\in\\left[1, \\infty^{n}\\right.}\\), and a set of \\(B\\) bins to discretize the time dimension, the timestamps are scaled to the range \\([0, B-1]\\), and the event volume is generated as:\n\\begin{aligned} t_{i}^{*} \u0026amp;=(B-1)\\left(t_{i}-t_{0}\\right) /\\left(t_{N}-t_{1}\\right) \\\\\\\nV(x, y, t) \u0026amp;=\\sum_{i} p_{i} k_{b}\\left(x-x_{i}\\right) k_{b}\\left(y-y_{i}\\right) k_{b}\\left(t-t_{i}^{*}\\right) \\\\\\\nk_{b}(a) \u0026amp;=\\max (0,1-|a|) \\end{aligned}\nwhere \\(k_{b}(a)\\) is the bilinear sampling kernel.\n\u0026lt;biblio.bib\u0026gt;\n", 
        "url": "https:\/\/braindump.jethro.dev\/posts\/zhu_unsupervised_2018\/"
    },
    
    
    
    "https:\/\/braindump.jethro.dev\/posts\/now\/": {
        
        "title": "🌿 What I\x27m Doing Now",
        "tags": [],
        "content": "This is a regularly updated log of what I\u0026rsquo;m currently looking into.\n High Performance Natural Language Processing. The research conducted here is part of my day job. Working on Org-roam, as usual. Developmental progress on it has sort of stalled. Now that I\u0026rsquo;m getting back into research, the flaws and things I want are starting to show, and I want to sit on my workflow a bit more before upgrading Org-roam to fit it better. Pruning this digital garden. There are a lot of unnecessary notes. It always feels wasteful to delete notes, but I think it will eventually be for the best. Thinking about the tension between plain text and the multi-media experience we have (Plain Text Is Not Enough)  ", 
        "url": "https:\/\/braindump.jethro.dev\/posts\/now\/"
    },
